title: Chinese fab SMIC's 7nm metal pitch beats Intel 18A but lags 38% on density, teardown finds — Huawei's sanctions-beating HiSilicon Kirin 9030 is the first subject of SemiAnalysis's new teardown lab
blurb: SemiAnalysis published the first teardown from its new Hillsboro lab, finding that SMIC's third-gen 7nm process in Huawei's Kirin 9030 hits a 32.5nm minimum metal pitch, tighter than the 36nm shipping in Intel's 18A Panther Lake chips. Achieved without EUV via DUV quadruple-patterning, the node still trails Intel's 18A high-density library by 38% and leaves the Kirin's prime core performing roughly level with 2021-era Arm cores.

title: A Red-Team Study of Anthropic Fable 5 & Opus 4.8 Models
blurb: A red-team study using the HackAgent framework tested Anthropic's Fable 5 and Opus 4.8 against four jailbreak families across 7,826 harmful intents, with every apparent success re-adjudicated by a three-judge panel. Both models resisted most attacks, but adaptive tree-of-attacks search broke Opus 4.8 on 11.5% of intents and Fable 5 on 6.1%, yielding 1,620 and 702 confirmed harmful completions located cheaply with no human in the loop.

title: GhostTree Attack Abused Recursive Windows Junctions to Hide Malware
blurb: Researchers detailed GhostTree, an attack that uses recursive Windows NTFS junctions to create infinite file paths that stall EDR scans and hide malware.

title: OptinMonster WordPress plugin hacked in CDN supply-chain attack
blurb: Three popular WordPress plugins from Awesome Motive—OptinMonster, TrustPulse, and PushEngage—were compromised after attackers stole the company's CDN API key and injected malicious JavaScript into scripts loaded by millions of sites, including the 1.2 million using OptinMonster. The malware harvested admin authentication tokens to create rogue accounts and install a self-hiding backdoor with web-shell and arbitrary PHP execution, granting full remote control of affected websites.

title: Ethereum’s biggest protocol overhaul in years moves into its final development stage
blurb: Ethereum developers have entered the final development phase of Glamsterdam, the network's largest upgrade since the 2022 Merge, running internal devnets with the full slate of planned EIPs before moving to public testnets. Headline changes include enshrined Proposer-Builder Separation (EIP-7732), which moves block-building onchain to curb MEV manipulation, with activation expected in the second half of the year.

title: Cybercriminals mask malicious communications through Microsoft Teams relays
blurb: The DragonForce ransomware group deployed custom malware called Backdoor.Turn that hides command-and-control traffic inside legitimate Microsoft Teams TURN relay infrastructure, making outbound connections look like normal traffic to Microsoft servers, according to Symantec. The technique kept attackers on a US services company's network for one to two months and marks the first known abuse of Teams relay infrastructure in the wild.

title: CISA warns of another cPanel plugin flaw exploited in attacks
blurb: CISA gave federal agencies three days to patch an actively exploited LiteSpeed cPanel plugin flaw (CVE-2026-54420).

title: Windows version of SprySOCKS Linux malware used to attack govt orgs
blurb: Researchers found Windows variants of the SprySOCKS Linux malware in attacks on government organizations across at least four countries.

title: Bug in FIFA World Cup internal system gave anyone ability to modify TV stream
blurb: A security researcher known as BobDaHacker found that registering as a player agent on FIFA's official platform, combined with a back-end API that never verified authorization, granted access to internal systems controlling World Cup TV streams and commentator displays. FIFA patched the flaw within hours of Tuesday's report but never acknowledged the researcher, who noted an attacker could have hijacked every broadcast camera at once.

title: Looped World Models
blurb: LoopWM introduces looped world models that iteratively refine latent states through a parameter-shared transformer block, claiming up to 100x parameter efficiency.

title: Wolfram Language and Mathematica Version 15, AI Assistant, Symbolic Music, More
blurb: Wolfram released Version 15 of Wolfram Language and Mathematica, adding built-in AI assistance, symbolic music representation, and dedicated interfaces designed for AI systems alongside human users. The release leans into the idea that the language's coherent design now serves AIs as well as people, giving them a precise way to formalize and verify what natural-language prompts actually compute.

title: New Rokarolla Android Malware Steals PINs, SMS Codes, and Crypto Wallet Funds
blurb: Zimperium documented Rokarolla, an Android trojan targeting 217 banking and crypto apps with 137 remote commands to steal PINs, SMS codes, and wallet funds.

title: SpaceX is public: Everything you need to know post-IPO
blurb: SpaceX priced its IPO at $135 per share to raise $85.7 billion, the largest offering in history, making Elon Musk the world's first trillionaire. Shares jumped 19% on their June 12 debut and have since pushed the company's valuation to $2.7 trillion, passing Amazon, while a $60B all-stock deal to acquire Cursor followed days later.

title: Introducing Brazos: Bringing liquid cooling to air-cooled data centers
blurb: Google released Brazos, a rack-mounted closed-loop liquid-to-air cooling system that lets operators run high-density AI chips exceeding 1000W TDP inside existing air-cooled data centers without facility-wide chilled-water retrofits. Now generally available, each three-unit setup cools a 60kW rack, installs one rack at a time on standard OCP ORv3 hardware, and isolates its internal coolant loop from the facility water supply.

title: Android 17 launches with new multitasking tools as Google expands Gemini features
blurb: Google released the final version of Android 17 alongside Wear OS 7 and a Pixel Drop, leading with a new "bubble bar" multitasking UI and an expanded set of Gemini features including the Lyria 3 music model, multimodal Gemini Omni video editing, and AudioLM speech translation. The release underscores Google's strategy of using Pixel as a showcase for its latest AI, as Apple races to catch up with Siri and iOS 27 upgrades due in September.

title: Elon Musk Is Unleashing SpaceX’s New War Chest to Solve His AI Problem
blurb: Elon Musk plans to use SpaceX's cash reserves to fund an AI push, including a reported $60B acquisition of coding agent Cursor and a move into renting out data-center capacity. The strategy would give SpaceX a foothold in enterprise AI, positioning the rocket company to court business customers and compete against Musk's own xAI and established cloud providers.

title: SpaceX to acquire AI coding platform Cursor for $60 billion
blurb: SpaceX will acquire AI coding tool Cursor for $60B in an all-stock deal expected to close in Q3, two days after SpaceX's IPO and months after its merger with xAI. Cursor's revenue grew over the past year but its market share slipped as Anthropic's Claude Code took the lead, and the deal deepens an existing xAI tie that gave Cursor compute and joint model training.

title: Flock Cameras Are Being Used for Stalking
blurb: Schneier documents more than a dozen cases nationwide where police officers used the Flock automated license-plate-reader network to illegally track and stalk people, including ex-partners and other private individuals. The piece illustrates a structural flaw in mass surveillance systems: once the infrastructure exists, insider abuse becomes a matter of access rather than capability, and oversight rarely catches it in time.

title: Catastrophic Forgetting is Low-Rank: A Function-Space Theory for Continual Adaptation
blurb: Catastrophic forgetting concentrates in a few NTK eigenmodes, giving a closed-form predictor for which outputs drift during continual adaptation.

title: SprySOCKS Windows Variant Abuses Kernel Drivers to Evade Detection
blurb: ESET found a Windows version of the China-linked SprySOCKS backdoor using kernel drivers to hide on government targets across four countries.

title: CISA Warns of Actively Exploited Joomla JCE Flaw Allowing PHP Code Execution
blurb: CISA added a maximum-severity Joomla Content Editor flaw (CVE-2026-48907, CVSS 10.0) to its KEV catalog, enabling unauthenticated PHP code execution.

title: Boosting MoE Training Throughput with Advanced Fusion Kernels
blurb: Nvidia released advanced fused MLP kernels for dense and mixture-of-experts models, built with its CuTe DSL to eliminate activation, CPU-synchronization, and quantization bottlenecks. The kernels deliver 1.3x–2x kernel-level speedups and contribute 8% end-to-end gains for DeepSeek-V3 and 93% for GPT-OSS pre-training, available now through cuDNN Frontend, Transformer Engine, and Megatron-Core.

title: Feds freaked over Fable 5 after simple 'fix this code' prompt, not jailbreak, says researcher
blurb: A security researcher says federal alarm over the "Fable 5" incident stemmed from a routine "fix this code" prompt rather than any deliberate jailbreak of the model. The account suggests the system surfaced dangerous output through ordinary use, raising questions about whether current safety reviews can distinguish genuine attacks from benign developer requests that happen to trip a model's guardrails.

title: Introducing OpenSharing: the Next Evolution of Delta Sharing for the Agentic Era
blurb: Databricks announced OpenSharing, an open-source protocol that extends Delta Sharing beyond tables and files to models, agents, and AI skills across any cloud, vendor, and format. It natively supports Delta Lake, Apache Iceberg, and Parquet so data stays in place, addressing the agentic-era need to exchange semantic context and autonomous agents across organizational boundaries that vendor-locked protocols handle poorly.

title: Ternary Mamba: Grouped Quantization-Aware Training of W1.58A16 State Space Models
blurb: Ternary Mamba shows that ternary state space models can be created from a pretrained Mamba-2 checkpoint rather than trained from scratch, cutting the token budget 1,000x by using grouped quantization-aware training with distillation from a frozen FP16 teacher. The method compresses Mamba-2 1.3B by 3.61x (2,687 to 744 MB) and hits 48.1% zero-shot accuracy in 102M tokens and four H100 GPU-hours.

title: The time the x86 emulator team found code so bad they fixed it during emulation
blurb: A Microsoft engineer recounted how an early x86-32 binary-translation emulator encountered a program whose compiler "optimized" a 64KB stack initialization by unrolling the loop into 65,536 individual byte-write instructions, consuming 256KB of code to zero out 64KB of data. The emulation team was so offended that they added special detection logic to the translator to recognize the bloated function and swap in an equivalent tight loop at translation time.

title: Snap finally debuts its long-awaited AR glasses, Specs, and, oof, they aren’t cheap
blurb: Snap released Specs, its consumer AR glasses, at $2,195, with preorders opening June 16 and shipping this fall in the US, UK, and France. The standalone device runs two Snapdragon processors with no tether, offers contextual AI lookups and shared multiplayer sessions, and undercuts Apple's Vision Pro while pricing well above most Meta Ray-Bans.

title: Google Vertex AI SDK Flaw Let Attackers Hijack Model Uploads via Bucket Squatting
blurb: Google patched a vulnerability in the Vertex AI SDK for Python that let an attacker hijack a victim's machine learning model uploads using only the target's often-public project ID, then run code inside Google's serving infrastructure. Palo Alto Networks Unit 42, which reported the "Pickle in the Middle" bug and saw no exploitation in the wild, recommends updating to version 1.148.0 or later.

title: OpenAI prepares major ChatGPT voice upgrade with GPT-Bidi-1
blurb: OpenAI is preparing GPT-Bidi-1, a next-generation audio model that would deliver ChatGPT's biggest voice upgrade in months, built on a bidirectional architecture that listens and speaks at once and adjusts mid-sentence instead of freezing on interruptions. Signs across web and mobile point to a near consumer rollout, with users toggling between a new Bidi (Latest) mode and current Advanced Voice Mode across High, Medium, and Instant intelligence tiers.

title: Anywhere, Any-Stymie: Remote Activation of Trojan Malware on LiDAR with Modulated Signals
blurb: Researchers demonstrated dormant malware embedded in LiDAR firmware that stays inactive during normal operation and can be triggered remotely by an optical signal, without hardware or network access at attack time. Once activated, it manipulates point clouds in real time to inject fake objects or suppress real ones, working at 300 feet and on vehicles moving up to 35 mph, with safety-critical effects shown on a deployed autonomous vehicle.

title: Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization: Teacher in Prompts, Not Gradients
blurb: ZPPO injects a teacher's guidance through prompts instead of the policy gradient, beating distillation and GRPO most at small student scales.

title: Verifying the Rust Standard Library
blurb: A crowdsourced campaign added static verification to the Rust standard library, targeting the unsafe code that the language's type system can't guarantee and that was previously checked only through testing and Miri. The authors describe what they call the largest verification effort reported for a software library, producing machine-checked proofs against specific undefined behaviors like out-of-bounds access, null pointer dereferences, and use of uninitialized memory.

title: NVIDIA Blackwell Tops MLPerf Training 6.0 with Industry-Leading Scale and Performance
blurb: NVIDIA's Blackwell platform swept MLPerf Training v6.0, posting the fastest time-to-train at scale and the highest per-accelerator performance on every benchmark while being the only platform to submit on all tests. Partners scaled to 8,192 Blackwell GPUs across cloud data centers, and the GB300 NVL72 system led the two new pretraining workloads based on the 671B-parameter DeepSeek-V3 and GPT-OSS-20B MoE models.

title: Lutnick’s Letter to Anthropic Warned of Curbs on Top AI Models
blurb: US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick warned Anthropic in a letter last week that it must obtain government permission before granting foreign nationals access to its most advanced AI models, according to a copy seen by Bloomberg. The letter threatened criminal and civil penalties for noncompliance, signaling a sharp escalation in federal efforts to control who can use frontier AI systems.

title: Qualcomm said to be circling AI chip biz Tenstorrent in $10B RISC-V power play
blurb: Qualcomm is reportedly in talks to acquire AI chip startup Tenstorrent, the RISC-V company led by chip architect Jim Keller, in a deal valued at roughly $10B. The acquisition would mark a major bet on the open RISC-V instruction set architecture, giving Qualcomm a foothold in AI accelerators as it pushes beyond its mobile-chip core into data center silicon.

title: Riding High After I.P.O., SpaceX Will Buy A.I. Start-Up for $60 Billion
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire AI coding startup Cursor for $60B in an all-stock deal, exercising an option just after its own IPO. The purchase pushes Elon Musk deeper into artificial intelligence, adding Cursor's developer tooling to a portfolio that already spans rockets, satellites, and xAI's model work.

title: Visual Verification Enables Inference-time Steering and Autonomous Policy Improvement
blurb: VERITAS pairs a pretrained generalist robot policy with a gradient-free visual verifier that scores candidate actions at inference time, improving task performance without additional training. Fine-tuning the policy on these verified self-generated trajectories matched the efficiency of expert demonstrations while requiring no human intervention, pointing to a scalable way for deployed robots to improve from their own experience.

title: Stanford's DeLM cuts multi-agent task costs 50% — without a central orchestrator
blurb: Stanford researchers introduced DeLM, a decentralized language model framework that lets agents coordinate through a shared knowledge base instead of routing every update through a central orchestrator. By letting agents build directly on one another's verified progress, DeLM avoids the communication bottleneck of a main agent and cuts multi-agent task costs by roughly 50%.

title: Compositional Skill Routing for LLM Agents: Decompose, Retrieve, and Compose
blurb: SkillWeaver decomposes complex agent queries into atomic sub-tasks, retrieves a skill for each, and composes an executable plan, cutting context use by over 99%.

title: NoiseTilt: Noise-Tilted Reverse Kernels for Diffusion Reward Alignment
blurb: NoiseTilt guides diffusion sampling by tilting the noise term instead of the mean, hitting top reward quality at 25 NFEs versus 500.

title: Seeing Is Not Screening: Multimodal Hidden Instruction Attacks on Agent Skill Scanners
blurb: Researchers hide malicious instructions inside images bundled with agent skills, evading text-based scanners that don't analyze visual content.

title: Humiliating IIS servers for fun and jail time
blurb: A bug bounty hunter walks through how to find and exploit Microsoft IIS web servers, arguing their default splash page often signals a deeper trove of misconfigurations. The guide covers Shodan and Google dorking to locate IIS boxes, header fingerprinting at scale, and techniques like HTTP parameter pollution to bypass web application firewalls.

title: Learning from the Self-future: On-policy Self-distillation for dLLMs
blurb: d-OPSD adapts on-policy self-distillation to diffusion LLMs, beating RLVR and SFT baselines using roughly 10% of the optimization steps.

title: Snap unveils $2,195 AR glasses as CEO Evan Spiegel bets on post-smartphone future
blurb: Snap released Specs, its first consumer AR glasses, priced at $2,195 with CEO Evan Spiegel framing them as a post-smartphone computing bet that ships later this year in the US, UK, and France. The device puts Snap against better-capitalized rivals Meta and Google, whose ad businesses fund hardware experiments, while Snap has lost money every year as a public company and saw shares fall 4% on the news.

title: Wear OS 7 helps your smartwatch keep up with you
blurb: Google released Wear OS 7, rolling out to eligible Pixel Watch devices today with Android Live Updates on the wrist, cross-device media and accessory controls, and up to 10% better battery life over Wear OS 6. Select devices get Gemini Intelligence features later this year, including natural-language widget creation, multi-step app automation, and Personal Intelligence that pulls from Gmail and Search.

title: AMD's Mext buy shows how AI could solve the RAM shortage it created
blurb: AMD acquired Mext, a startup building software that uses AI to manage memory more efficiently, as the industry contends with a RAM shortage driven partly by AI demand. The deal positions AMD to ease pressure on system memory through smarter data tiering and allocation, addressing a supply crunch that frontier AI workloads helped create in the first place.

title: Uncertainty Quantification for Flow-Based Vision-Language-Action Models
blurb: A method quantifies uncertainty in flow-based vision-language-action robotics models, enabling failure detection and adaptation with 22% fewer demonstrations.

title: Openrouter Fusion API
blurb: OpenRouter's Fusion routes each request to the best-performing model automatically, blending providers behind a single API endpoint.

title: Databricks says it solved the decades-old data pipeline problem that's been slowing AI agents
blurb: Databricks announced Lakehouse//RT and LTAP at its Data + AI Summit, two products aimed at collapsing the operational and analytical data stack that has long sat between AI agents and live data. Lakehouse//RT brings millisecond query latency to governed Delta and Iceberg tables, while LTAP stores Postgres transactional data in those formats at write time, removing the ETL pipelines and dedicated serving tier enterprises have maintained for decades.

title: Import AI 461: "Alignment is not on track"; FrontierCode; and synthetic research interns
blurb: Researchers from the UK AI Security Institute's alignment team and the startup Timaeus have formed Sequent, a nonprofit pursuing alignment techniques for superintelligent systems because "alignment is not on track." Sequent aims to raise $100–150M initially and reach 40–80 employees within a couple of years, betting on a portfolio of theory-driven approaches rather than the reactive empirical methods it ascribes to frontier labs.

title: Trump-backed World Liberty Financial nears OCC approval for federal trust charter: report
blurb: World Liberty Financial, the Trump-backed crypto firm, nears OCC approval for a federal trust charter to issue its USD1 stablecoin.

title: UK to require ID or face scan before you can make social media accounts
blurb: The UK will ban under-16s from social media starting spring 2027, requiring platforms like Instagram, TikTok, YouTube, and X to verify ages by checking government ID or running facial age scans on anyone opening a new account. Long-standing accounts are largely exempt, but the rules effectively end anonymous signups in the UK, and security experts warn the checks are easy to bypass while exposing ID and biometric data to breaches.

title: Announcing Apps on Databricks Marketplace
blurb: Databricks announced the Public Preview of Apps on Databricks Marketplace, letting customers discover, install, and run third-party data and AI applications natively inside their Databricks workspaces. The apps deploy within Unity Catalog so data stays in the customer's environment with no egress, while providers gain a no-egress distribution channel to monetize proprietary tools across thousands of enterprises.

title: Databricks says annualized revenue rose 80%+ YoY to $6.9B, up from $5.4B in Q4; CEO Ali Ghodsi says higher AI agent usage is increasing costs, lowering margins (Jordan Novet/CNBC)
blurb: Databricks said annualized revenue rose more than 80% year over year to $6.9B, up from $5.4B in the prior quarter. CEO Ali Ghodsi said rising AI agent usage is driving up compute costs and pressuring margins, a sign that heavier inference workloads are reshaping the economics even as demand accelerates.

title: Meta launches AI Mode in Facebook search to answer questions
blurb: Meta launched AI Mode in Facebook Search, which generates answers directly from public content across Facebook Groups, Reels, and other Meta apps instead of returning a standard list of results. Powered by Meta AI and Muse Spark, the feature turns Facebook's social posts into a discovery engine for recommendations and advice, though Meta has not explained how it selects which posts, Groups, or Reels appear in responses.

title: Kodak confirms data breach claimed by ShinyHunters extortion gang
blurb: Kodak confirmed it is investigating a breach with external cybersecurity experts after an unauthorized party gained temporary access to a limited amount of company data. The ShinyHunters extortion gang claimed responsibility, saying it stole over 2.2 million records of customer PII and internal data, and set a June 18 deadline to leak the files unless Kodak pays.

title: FBI dismantles Chinese phishing service that coached buyers to generate scam sites using AI —$88 cybercrime product linked to $1.9 billion in losses, 3.87 million stolen cards
blurb: The FBI, Google, and Lumen Technologies dismantled Outsider Enterprise, a China-based phishing-as-a-service operation that sold a $88-per-week kit for spinning up fake bank, toll, and delivery pages, seizing its servers and payment wallets. Google's complaint alleges operators handed subscribers tutorials for prompting Gemini to write phishing-page code disguised as innocuous "gift redemption" requests, linking the platform to 3.87 million stolen cards and $1.9 billion in losses since July 2023.

title: WEQA: Wearable hEalth Question Answering with Query-Adaptive Agentic Reasoning
blurb: WEQA is a query-adaptive agent framework that routes wearable health questions to specialized sensor tools, scoring 24% higher than LLM and agentic baselines.

title: Typst 0.15.0
blurb: Typst 0.15.0 adds variable font support, native HTML equation export via MathML, and an experimental bundle target that lets one project output multiple files like a multi-page website. The release also lets documents hold multiple bibliographies and target several PDF standards at once, and it switches file paths to forward slashes only as a breaking change.

title: Google Flight Simulator
blurb: Google Earth on web added an experimental flight simulator that lets users fly around the world using keyboard controls, with 3D buildings and high-resolution imagery streaming in dynamically. It uses simplified physics aimed at casual exploration rather than aerodynamic training, includes crash recovery that restarts the aircraft at a safe altitude, and is currently available only in the browser.

title: Unlocking UK house-building with AI-accelerated planning
blurb: Google DeepMind is partnering with the UK government to build a Gemini-powered prototype that helps planning officers process householder applications, aiming to cut decision times by 50%. Co-developed with Google Cloud, Faculty, and councils in Barnet, Camden, and Dorset, the tool automates data extraction and case analysis on routine cases like loft conversions, with a national rollout planned for 2027.

title: DOJ seizes CFAKE, SOCFAKE deepfake nude sites under TAKE IT DOWN Act
blurb: The DOJ seized the CFAKE.com and SOCFAKE.com domains, which allegedly hosted nonconsensual AI-generated nude images and videos of women, marking the first publicly announced domain seizure under the TAKE IT DOWN Act. The action signals that federal enforcement of the new law is moving from statute to practice, putting deepfake-image operators on notice that hosting infrastructure is now a direct target.

title: Anthropic's Fable shutdown is a big moment for open-source AI
blurb: Anthropic disabled its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models for all customers to comply with a US export control directive citing national security, cutting off companies that had built on them. The abrupt shutoff is driving interest in downloadable open-source models that run on a company's own infrastructure, where no political fight can switch them off, with Chinese labs MiniMax and Zhipu surging on the news.

title: Introducing OpenSharing SecureConnect
blurb: Databricks introduced OpenSharing SecureConnect, a managed proxy that routes storage access on behalf of recipients so data providers no longer configure firewall rules for each one. Providers do a one-time setup to allowlist Databricks endpoints, data stays in their own storage bucket, and an optional Network Connectivity Configuration adds private link connectivity between the proxy and provider storage.

title: June Pixel Drop: New features for creators, Gemini upgrades and more
blurb: Google released its June 2026 Pixel Drop, adding Screen reactions for built-in selfie-camera recording, Gemini Omni video editing, Gemini 3 music generation, and floating Bubbles multitasking, alongside Android 17 and Wear OS 7. The update leans heavily on generative AI features tied to Gemini, positioning Pixel as the showcase device for Google's latest models as they roll out over the coming weeks.

title: Coinbase introduces AI adviser, stock options, and pre-IPO markets in finance push
blurb: Coinbase introduced a broad suite of products spanning stock and ETF trading, equity and crypto options, pre-IPO perpetual futures, an SEC-registered AI adviser, and consumer banking features as it pushes to become an "everything exchange." The expansion puts the largest US crypto exchange in direct competition with brokerages, banks, and fintechs, with pre-IPO contracts tied to OpenAI and Anthropic expected to follow.

title: Security and Privacy Prompts in the Wild: What Users Ask LLMs and How LLMs Respond
blurb: Analyzing 14,727 real security and privacy prompts from WildChat, this paper finds GPT-5.5 gives adequate answers 98% of the time versus Llama 4's 47%, but commercial models still contradict themselves across repeated runs.

title: Correlated randomness in Slay the Spire 2
blurb: Slay the Spire 2 uses separate pseudorandom number generators seeded from the same value plus a per-purpose hash, but a developer found they stay correlated, so the first output of one RNG leaks information about the first output of all the others. This produces strange measurable effects, like a curse being 54% likely to be Debt or first fights dropping potions 76% of the time in one area but 4% in another.

title: From pixels to planning: Earth AI for nature restoration
blurb: Google Research released a vectorized dataset of fine-scale woody features like hedgerows, stone walls, and copses across the UK, built with a high-resolution deep learning framework that detects ecological structures too small for standard satellite imaging. Converting earlier pixel-based Farmscapes maps into actionable vector data lets landowners and conservationists measure and expand carbon-storing habitat without displacing cropland or compromising food security.

title: Non-x86 servers now nearly half the market, IDC says
blurb: Non-x86 servers now make up nearly half the global server market, IDC says, driven by surging AI system demand and a shortage of DRAM and NAND memory. The shift reflects how Arm-based and accelerator-heavy designs are reshaping data center buildouts, even as memory constraints squeeze supply and pricing across the broader market.

title: AI governance at Data + AI Summit 2026: What’s new with Unity AI Gateway
blurb: At Data + AI Summit 2026, Databricks expanded Unity AI Gateway, its governance layer for enterprise AI, with new controls for cost, access, and monitoring across models, agents, MCP services, and skills. The update adds spend visibility across providers, hard spend caps, intelligent routing, and unified tracing, extending Unity Catalog governance to the runtime interactions between agents and the tools they use.

title: Android 17 starts hitting Pixel phones and watches today
blurb: Google began rolling out the official Android 17 build to Pixel phones today, alongside a Pixel Drop and a new Wear OS version based on Android 17 for Pixel Watches. The headline addition is Bubbles, a multitasking system that opens any app as a floating window via long-press and docks into a bubble bar on foldables, though Pixels remain the only devices running Android 17 for now.

title: [AINews] GLM-5.2: the top Frontend Coding model in the world, IndexShare for Speculative Decoding
blurb: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, an MIT-licensed 744B-parameter open-weight model that third-party leaderboards now rank as the top frontend coding model, ahead of every Opus release including 4.8. The model ships with a 1M-token context window, two reasoning-effort modes, and a tweak to DeepSeek Sparse Attention that improves efficiency at ultra-long contexts.

title: “성능 향상 vs 데이터 우려”…스페이스X의 커서 인수에 엇갈린 평가
blurb: SpaceX confirmed plans to acquire AI coding startup Cursor for $60B in an all-stock deal, with a $10B breakup fee if SpaceX walks away. Analysts are split: the deal gives Cursor access to xAI's GPU and datacenter capacity that has capped its growth, but enterprise CIOs worry about data trust, model choice beyond Grok, and rising competition from AWS Kiro, Google, and Microsoft.

title: Tesco is sprinting to quit VMware and Broadcom despite rapid migration risks
blurb: Tesco is racing to migrate off VMware and Broadcom virtualization, accepting the risks of a rapid cutover to escape Broadcom's restructured licensing and steep cost increases. The UK grocery giant joins a growing list of large enterprises abandoning the stack since Broadcom's acquisition, signaling that pricing pressure is now outweighing the operational danger of moving critical infrastructure on a compressed timeline.

title: AMD acquires storage software startup MEXT
blurb: AMD acquired storage software startup MEXT to fold its AI-powered predictive memory technology into the EPYC data center portfolio. MEXT's software-only Predictive Memory makes flash appear as DRAM to the operating system, expanding usable memory capacity to cut server costs as DRAM prices climb. Terms were not disclosed for the Santa Clara startup, which had raised $10.6M since 2023.

title: Why Weibo’s tiny VibeThinker-3B has the AI world arguing over benchmarks again
blurb: Weibo researchers released VibeThinker-3B, a 3B-parameter reasoning model that scored 94.3 on AIME 2026, matching DeepSeek V3.2 (671B parameters) and edging past Google's Gemini 3 Pro at 91.7. The result has split the AI community between those calling it evidence that small models can rival giants and skeptics who argue the benchmarks themselves have become gameable.

title: Trust the Right Teacher: Quality-Aware Self-Distillation for GUI Grounding
blurb: A new self-distillation method for GUI grounding improves coordinate accuracy by down-weighting unreliable teacher signals and calibrating with teacher confidence.

title: SpaceX to acquire vibe coding startup Cursor for $60B
blurb: SpaceX announced plans to acquire Cursor, the AI coding startup officially known as Anysphere, for $60 billion in stock, with the deal expected to close by quarter's end. The price reflects Cursor's growth to more than 1 million daily active users and follows an April partnership in which SpaceX supplied tens of thousands of GPUs to train Cursor's in-house Composer 2.5 model.

title: Unintended Effects of Geographic Conditioning in Large Language Models
blurb: Injecting location metadata into prompts makes LLMs leak region-specific references up to 793x above baseline, even when the location is "Unknown".

title: Can Europe train a frontier AI model on the compute it owns?
blurb: EuroMesh inventories the GPU capacity Europe controls and argues the continent could assemble enough compute to train a frontier-scale model without relying on US clouds, if fragmented public and private clusters were pooled. The project frames the real obstacle as coordination and interconnect rather than raw hardware, putting a number on what a federated European training run would actually require.

title: Prop For That
blurb: Adam Argyle released Prop For That, a CSS library that exposes browser data JavaScript normally tracks (cursor position, scroll velocity, form states, current time) as live custom properties. Importing the library and adding a `data-props-for` attribute generates values like `--live-pointer-x` that can be dropped straight into stylesheets, handling the scripting in the background so interactions are authored in plain CSS.

title: First Proof Second Batch
blurb: A new paper tested several AI systems on ten research-level mathematics problems, each drawn from the actual research work of contributing mathematicians across fields like number theory, geometry, and probability. The authors release the problems, human solutions, AI-generated solutions, and referee reports for each, providing a benchmark for how well current models handle open-ended math that resists pattern matching.

title: From Reasoning Traces to Reusable Modules: Understanding Compositional Generalization in Language Model Reasoning
blurb: This paper formalizes how SFT and RL play complementary roles in LLM reasoning, with RL extracting reusable atomic modules from the compound traces SFT supplies.

title: An Empirical Analysis of AI Slop in Music Streaming
blurb: A new paper measuring AI-generated music on streaming platforms finds that 93% of AI tracks receive few or no plays, as creators flood Spotify across genres hoping for a hit. The authors published their own AI songs through 11 indie distributors to expose weak, unenforced AI policies, and warn that falling generation costs will make music slop self-sustaining without industry intervention.

title: ICYMI: OpenAI released CDP support for browser use on Codex
blurb: OpenAI gave Codex Chrome DevTools Protocol access, letting the agent inspect performance, network traffic, and rewrite a page's DOM live.

title: Linux 7.1 arrives with an NTFS overhaul and major hardware performance boosts
blurb: Linus Torvalds released Linux 7.1, a stable kernel featuring a new NTFS driver, Intel FRED support for better performance on Panther Lake CPUs, and faster Intel Arc Battlemage graphics. The release also improves support for older AMD Radeon GPUs and folds in fixes across USB, graphics, and networking drivers, so hardware that previously failed under Linux may now work on a 7.1-based distro.

title: TSMC says panel packaging won't replace CoWoS anytime soon for the largest future AI processors — wafer-level tech can scale to 58 massive dies in one package
blurb: TSMC said panel-level packaging will not replace its wafer-level CoWoS technology anytime soon for the largest AI processors, citing far lower interconnection density despite panels enabling bigger package sizes. The company said its CoWoS roadmap can scale to 14X reticle size and integrate up to 58 large reticle-sized dies in a single package, leaving substantial runway before panel-based methods become necessary.

title: Security Community Slams US Ban on Exporting Mythos, Fable
blurb: Dozens of security experts signed an open letter urging the US government to reverse export controls that bar foreign nationals from using Anthropic's newly launched Claude Mythos 5 and Fable 5 models. Anthropic suspended both models for all customers on June 12 to comply with the order, which reportedly followed White House suspicions that a China-linked group accessed Mythos, a model capable of discovering critical software vulnerabilities and writing novel exploits.

title: A look at Wyoming's Frontier Stable Token, which launched in January and currently has a market value of ~$1M, as other states explore "white labeling" the tech (David Voreacos/Bloomberg)
blurb: Wyoming's state-backed Frontier Stable Token has reached a ~$1M market value since January, with other states exploring white-labeling the tech.

title: Sources: a new AI lab founded by Junyang Lin, former lead of Alibaba's Qwen models, has raised several hundred million dollars at a ~$2B post-money valuation (The Information)
blurb: Junyang Lin, former lead of Alibaba's Qwen model team, has founded a new AI lab that raised several hundred million dollars at a roughly $2B post-money valuation, according to The Information. The departure marks another high-profile researcher leaving a major lab to start an independent venture, and the early valuation underscores continued investor appetite for frontier AI teams.

title: Snap launches its AR glasses at $2,195 as a consumer product, betting the company on augmented reality
blurb: Snap launched the consumer version of its Specs AR glasses at $2,195, with preorders open now via a $200 deposit and shipping this fall in the US, UK, and France. The self-contained glasses run on two Snapdragon processors with a 51-degree field of view and four-hour battery, an expensive bet on a new hardware category from a company that recently cut 16% of its workforce.

title: Is It Real? Exploiting Virtual-Physical Discrimination Vulnerability in Mixed Reality
blurb: A new paper identifies the "virtual-physical discrimination vulnerability" in mixed reality headsets as an exploitable security flaw, since users often cannot distinguish virtual objects from real ones. Testing four proof-of-concept attacks on Apple Vision Pro with 26 participants, the authors found success rates of 85% to 100% in altering behavior, including biased purchases and misdirected navigation, with the stealthiest attacks proving hardest to detect.

title: Qualcomm’s latest chip hints that more powerful smart glasses could be on the way
blurb: Qualcomm announced the Snapdragon Reality Elite, an XR chip with a 60% GPU bump, 160% higher NPU performance, and support for 4.4K-per-eye displays at 90fps. It debuts in Xreal's Project Aura Android XR glasses this fall, promising sharper visuals, on-device AI for larger LLMs, and up to 20% better battery life while running 12°C cooler than the prior generation.

title: Stop Using JWTs
blurb: This post argues that JWTs should not be used for browser session management, since the specification was designed for short-lived tokens and stateless authentication cannot be done securely without server-side state. The author recommends regular cookie sessions instead, noting that stateless tokens add complexity and risk without real advantages, and that even Google reserves JWTs for single sign-on transport rather than user sessions.

title: What are git worktrees, and why should I use them?
blurb: Git worktrees let you check out multiple branches into separate sibling folders at once, so you can fix a hotfix in one window while your feature branch stays untouched in another, with no stashing or context switching. GitHub argues they have become newly relevant because AI tooling pushes developers to run many sessions in parallel, though each worktree needs its own copy of dependencies.

title: Making my TypeScript types 15.7x faster
blurb: The maintainer of Sury, a schema validation library, cut its TypeScript type-instantiation cost by up to 15.7x using a three-step recipe: pin types with tests, measure instantiation counts with ArkType's Attest tool, then optimize with Claude's help. The biggest gain landed on type extraction via S.Output, the operation paid for on every type alias derived from a schema, with baselines bakeable into CI to catch regressions.

title: Anthropic "pauses" token-based billing for its Claude Agent SDK
blurb: Anthropic paused a billing change that would have charged Claude Agent SDK usage at standard API rates rather than counting it against subscribers' more generous weekly Claude plan limits. The reversal, announced just as the change was set to take effect on June 15, preserves a setup where heavy users can extract far more value than the same spend on API fees would buy.

title: Sources: Amazon faces a possible FTC lawsuit alleging it misled advertisers; multiple state AGs are involved and the case could seek billions in civil penalties (Bloomberg)
blurb: Amazon could face an FTC lawsuit alleging it misled advertisers, according to sources, with multiple state attorneys general joining the case. The suit could seek billions in civil penalties, adding to the regulatory pressure already weighing on Amazon's ad business and its broader antitrust fights.

title: SpaceX valuation balloons to $2.6T, briefly passes Amazon
blurb: SpaceX briefly surpassed Amazon to become the world's fifth-most valuable company Tuesday, hitting a $2.9 trillion valuation before settling near $2.6 trillion. The newly public company has added roughly $1 trillion since Friday's IPO, fueled by its $60 billion acquisition of AI coding startup Cursor and compute-leasing deals with Anthropic and Google, despite posting a $4.9 billion loss on $18.7 billion in revenue last year.

title: 2021 Honda Civic infotainment system can be jailbroken via USB — flaw uses public Android test keys to install unauthorized apps, enables for 'EvilValet' attacks
blurb: A 2021 Honda Civic's infotainment system can be jailbroken via USB using public Android test keys, enabling "EvilValet" malware installs through brief physical access.

title: 'A signal of where power sits': Trump and world leaders joined by OpenAI, Anthropic, Google at G7
blurb: The CEOs of OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google DeepMind will join Trump and other G7 leaders for a lunch on AI risks, infrastructure, and sovereignty at the summit in Evian. The gathering comes as Anthropic negotiates with Washington over export controls on its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models, underscoring how heads of state now need cooperation from the executives actually building the technology.

title: What job interviews taught me about Kubernetes
blurb: Companies adopt Kubernetes less for scale than for uniform deployments, hireable lingua-franca knowledge, and traceable, auditable rollouts.

title: Sweeping Android 17 update brings new AI capabilities and features to Pixel smartphones
blurb: Google released Android 17, rolling out to Pixel devices first with new AI models, call-management tools, and a multitasking interface. The update adds Gemini Omni, Lyria 3, and AudioLM for speech, multimodal, and music tasks across apps, alongside a "Bubble Bar" for app switching, personalized voicemail greetings, and Wear OS and security upgrades.

title: China’s DeepSeek reportedly raises $7.4B in funding at $50B+ valuation
blurb: DeepSeek reportedly raised $7.4B at a valuation above $50B, making it China's most valuable AI startup, with founder Liang Wenfeng contributing $3B and Tencent weighing a $1.48B stake. The raise follows the cost-efficient reasoning models that rattled chip stocks in early 2025, and Microsoft is now reportedly testing a customized DeepSeek model in Copilot as a cheaper alternative to OpenAI and Anthropic.

title: New discoverable space setting in Google Chat
blurb: Google Chat added a "Discoverable" space access type, letting users find and request to join spaces while message history stays private until approved.

title: Towards Understanding and Measuring COGNITIVE ATROPHY in LLM Behaviour
blurb: A new benchmark measures "cognitive atrophy," whether mental-health chatbots foster user dependence over reflection, finding moderate-to-high atrophy across five LLMs.

title: Google’s June 2026 Pixel Drop arrives with floating app bubbles, screen reactions and many new AI tools
blurb: Google began rolling out Android 17 to eligible Pixel phones alongside its June 2026 Pixel Drop, headlined by Bubbles, a feature that keeps apps like Gemini, browsers, and calendars in floating windows over other apps. The update also adds Screen Reactions selfie overlays for screen recordings, Gemini Omni text-to-video generation for Gemini Pro subscribers, and Lyria 3 music generation, plus wider rollouts of Voice Translate and conversational photo editing.

title: Cyberattack sees crops kept in the ground
blurb: A ransomware attack on a farming supplier disrupted operations enough that crops were left unharvested in the ground.

title: How Roku fits into Fox's future — and what investors are missing about the deal
blurb: Fox agreed to acquire Roku for $22B, adding the leading US streaming platform, its first-party ad data, and a second free ad-supported service to a portfolio that already includes Tubi and Fox's linear networks. Fox stock fell 16% to a 52-week low on the news, as investors weighed the added debt against a pivot that would make the combined company the third-largest US player by share of viewing.

title: ReAge3D: Re-Aging 3D Faces with View Consistency
blurb: ReAge3D produces identity-preserving, view-consistent 3D face re-aging by propagating a 2D diffusion re-aging model outward from a frontal pivot view.

title: Apple plans to change its Hide My Email privacy feature that could make it less effective
blurb: Apple will move its Hide My Email addresses from @icloud.com to @private.icloud.com in the coming weeks, making the anonymized addresses distinguishable from regular iCloud accounts. The change lets apps and websites identify and block private sign-ups, and developers must update their filtering so forwarded mail to existing addresses keeps going through.

title: Reservations are open for XREAL AURA — plus, see more news from AWE 2026.
blurb: XREAL opened reservations for AURA, its first wired XR glasses running Google's Android XR and built on Qualcomm's Snapdragon Reality Elite Platform, with shipping planned for this fall. The announcement at AWE 2026 expands the Android XR hardware lineup alongside Samsung's Galaxy XR, as Google pushes the platform across multiple partners and form factors.

title: SMI's PCIe 6.0 SSD controller for consumer SSDs coming next year, but severe NAND shortages will get even worse in 2027 as AI data centers swallow supply — An interview with Silicon Motion's SVP Nelson Duann
blurb: Silicon Motion will ship its first PCIe 6.0 SSD controller for consumer drives next year, SVP Nelson Duann said in a Computex 2026 interview, even as NAND shortages driven by AI data center demand worsen. Duann warned that client storage supply may not recover for years, with conditions set to deteriorate further in 2027 as hyperscalers absorb production.

title: AMD’s massive SP7 socket for EPYC Venice and Intel’s gargantuan 9,324-pin socket for Diamond Rapids appear at Computex — SP7 and LGA9324-1 sockets will power the next generation of AI servers
blurb: AMD and Intel showed off their next-generation server CPU sockets at Computex 2026, with AMD's SP7 supporting up to 256-core EPYC "Venice" chips and Intel's 9,324-pin LGA9324-1 socket built for 192-core Xeon "Diamond Rapids." SP7 handles up to 16 DDR6 channels, 96 PCIe 6.0 lanes, and CPUs drawing as much as 1,400W, pushing vendors toward liquid cooling for the AI-server era.

title: All the latest news on Android 17, Wear OS 7, and Android XR
blurb: Google began rolling out Android 17 to Pixel phones today, headlined by Bubbles floating app windows, native gamepad remapping for foldables, and a dedicated bubble bar for multitasking on larger screens. Wear OS 7 is also shipping to Pixel Watch models with Live Updates and up to 10% better battery life, while Android XR glasses from the Google-Xreal collaboration open for preorder ahead of a fall launch.

title: Sources: Jeff Bezos is investing in Cambridge, UK-based CuspAI, which applies generative AI to material sciences, as part of a $400M round at a $2.6B valuation (Tim Bradshaw/Financial Times)
blurb: Jeff Bezos is investing in CuspAI, a Cambridge, UK-based startup applying generative AI to materials science, as part of a $400M round valuing the company at $2.6B. The funding signals continued investor appetite for AI applied beyond software, targeting the discovery of new materials for use in areas like carbon capture and energy storage.

title: Build On-Device AI Companions with the NVIDIA ACE Game Agent SDK and Unreal Engine 5 Plugins
blurb: NVIDIA released the ACE Game Agent SDK, an open source C/C++ framework for building on-device AI companions that run on RTX hardware.

title: Sources: the US has held off adding DeepSeek, CXMT, and 100+ other flagged companies to its Entity List, last updated in October, the longest gap in 10+ years (Karen Freifeld/Reuters)
blurb: The US has not added DeepSeek, memory-chip maker CXMT, and more than 100 other flagged companies to its trade Entity List, leaving the restrictions untouched since October, according to sources cited by Reuters. The roughly eight-month pause is the longest gap in over a decade, suggesting the administration is holding export-control leverage in reserve as it negotiates broader trade terms with China.

title: Firefox 152 understands “Sssh!”
blurb: Firefox 152 adds a Quick Action that mutes audio across every tab and window when users type "mute," "sssh," or "hush" into the address bar. The release also ships a redesigned Settings page, batch tab-sharing for grouped tabs, experimental JPEG XL support, and around 40 security fixes, arriving as Chrome 150 prepares to strip the last Manifest V2 ad-blocking workarounds.

title: QuEra, AWS target fault-tolerant quantum computing on Amazon Braket by 2028
blurb: QuEra Computing and AWS plan to make a fault-tolerant quantum computer available through Amazon Braket in 2028, one of the industry's most concrete timelines yet. QuEra's neutral-atom Libra system targets more than 256 error-corrected logical qubits and a 10⁻⁶ logical error rate, enough to run roughly 1 million logical operations alongside existing cloud HPC and AI workloads.

title: GPT‑NL: a sovereign language model for the Netherlands
blurb: TNO, SURF, and the Netherlands Forensic Institute are building GPT-NL, a Dutch language model trained entirely from scratch to keep control over its data, weights, and governance inside the Netherlands and Europe. The project publishes its source code as open source and releases model weights under a controlled license, positioning sovereignty and transparency as alternatives to dependence on US-based providers.

title: Opinion | Why Does Trump Hate Anthropic?
blurb: A Wall Street Journal opinion piece argues that the Trump administration's de facto ban on Anthropic's Fable 5 model, reportedly tied to Pentagon and China-policy disputes, sets a dangerous precedent for government interference in the AI market. The author warns that singling out one lab's model for exclusion politicizes procurement and could chill competition among US frontier developers.

title: UK Social Media Ban for Minors Has Privacy Experts Worried
blurb: The UK will ban under-16s from user-to-user social platforms like Instagram, TikTok, and X starting in early 2027, joining Australia and Canada in restricting minors' access. Privacy experts warn the rules will require age-verification systems that effectively impose identity checks on all users, turning a child-safety measure into broad surveillance infrastructure.

title: SpaceX’s planned $60 billion deal for Cursor raises questions for CIOs
blurb: SpaceX officially announced plans to acquire AI coding startup Cursor for $60B in stock, formalizing terms it floated in April including a $10B breakup fee. Analysts say the deal gives Cursor, used by 64% of Fortune 500 companies, access to xAI's GPUs and data centers, but it leaves CIOs and developers facing long-term uncertainty about the tool's roadmap and trustworthiness.

title: Qualcomm wants to be the chip inside whatever replaces your smartphone, and it just announced two products toward that end
blurb: Qualcomm announced Snapdragon Reality Elite, a mixed-reality platform that runs a 3-billion-parameter model on-device at 45 tokens per second, plus START, a turnkey hardware and software toolkit for AI smart glasses. CEO Cristiano Amon said the chipmaker is working on more than 40 AI wearables, betting that the next major computing platform will be glasses and jewelry rather than phones.

title: General Atlantic in Talks to Lead Round for China’s Kling AI
blurb: Kuaishou is in talks with General Atlantic to lead a first financing round for its Kling AI video arm, seeking $2B at an $18B valuation. The deal would secure a major US backer for the ChatGPT-style video generator ahead of a planned IPO, separating the fast-growing unit from its parent.

title: Apple releases Beats Studio Buds firmware update with important microphone security fix
blurb: Apple released firmware 1B211 for Beats Studio Buds, patching a flaw (CVE-2025-20701) that let nearby attackers eavesdrop through the microphone before pairing.

title: Sarvam Becomes India's Latest AI Unicorn
blurb: Sarvam AI raised $234M from investors including HCL Tech, Bessemer, and Khosla Ventures, becoming India's latest AI unicorn. The round adds to a wave of capital flowing into Indian AI startups building region-specific models, with co-founder Pratyush Kumar positioning the company around language and infrastructure tailored to local markets.

title: SanDisk Optimus GX Pro 8100 8TB SSD Review — the undisputed king of high-capacity PCIe 5.0 SSDs
blurb: SanDisk's Optimus GX Pro 8100 is a rebadged WD Black SN8100 at 8TB, delivering top PCIe 5.0 performance at a steep price.

title: Report: Microsoft to use AWS to help GitHub deal with a major surge in demand
blurb: Microsoft is tapping AWS to help GitHub absorb a 30x surge in demand driven by AI coding agents, after an October 2025 plan for 10x capacity proved insufficient and reliability strained into repeated outages. The move is a short-term fix while GitHub continues a full migration to Azure targeted for 2027, underscoring the infrastructure pressure behind the agentic-coding boom.

title: Windows 11 KB5094126, KB5093998 bugging out Office apps but it may not be Microsoft's fault
blurb: Microsoft confirmed that its latest Patch Tuesday updates for Windows 11 and Windows 10 can prevent third-party apps from launching Office programs or opening Office documents, often with no error message. The bug stems from OLE automation and affects tools like Zotero, Dentrix, and CCH Engagement, though Microsoft has not tied it to the broader OneDrive, BitLocker, and BSOD complaints users are reporting.

title: Noctua finally releases its first AIO coolers — prices start at $220 for 240mm, features the company’s legendary A-series fans
blurb: Noctua released its first AIO liquid coolers in 240mm, 360mm, and 420mm sizes, starting at $219.95 and featuring its A-series fans.

title: Linux kernel 7.1 sends Intel 486 support to silicon heaven
blurb: Linux kernel 7.1 drops support for Intel's 486 processor, retiring code paths for chips that first shipped in 1989. Maintainers cited the maintenance burden of preserving compatibility for hardware that almost no one still runs, freeing the kernel to assume modern instruction-set features by default.

title: The new Siri makes one of Apple's most convenient OS features a cumbersome mess
blurb: The author argues that Apple's AI-powered Siri overhaul has degraded Spotlight, turning a fast, reliable system search into a slower interface that force-feeds Apple Intelligence results. The piece compares the experience to Google's AI Overviews, contending that Apple buried a genuinely useful OS feature under summarization bloat that users did not ask for and cannot easily disable.

title: Learning Cardiac Electrophysiology Digital Twins Through Agentic Discovery of Hybrid Structure
blurb: LEADS is a framework that uses an LLM agent to automatically discover patient-specific hybrid physics-neural models for cardiac electrophysiology digital twins, treating model structure as a searchable action space rather than relying on experts to design it by hand. The agent iteratively selects, combines, and refines candidate models while gradient descent fits parameters, and it outperformed both human-designed hybrid models and prior LLM-based approaches on synthetic and real cardiac data.

title: Databricks acquires cyberattack detection startup Panther
blurb: Databricks announced plans to acquire Panther Labs, a startup whose AI platform detects cyberattacks by filtering noise from telemetry streams and scanning logs against customer-defined detections. Terms weren't disclosed, but Panther was valued at $1.4B in a 2021 round backed by Snowflake Ventures and Coatue, and Databricks will fold its technology into its own breach-detection capabilities.

title: Sources: Samsung is seeing a rise in advanced chip production requests from BYD, Google, AMD, and others as TSMC's capacity remains constrained by AI demand (Nikkei Asia)
blurb: Samsung is fielding a rise in advanced chip production requests from BYD, Google, AMD, and others as TSMC's capacity stays constrained by AI demand, according to Nikkei Asia. The overflow gives Samsung's foundry a chance to win customers it has long struggled to attract, though it must prove its yields can match TSMC's on leading-edge nodes.

title: Sources: Meta decided to support KOSA after it was packaged with language preempting state AI laws and a bill requiring app stores to verify users' ages (Politico)
blurb: Meta has decided to back the Kids Online Safety Act after the bill was bundled with provisions that preempt state AI laws and a requirement for app stores to verify users' ages, according to Politico sources. The shift aligns Meta with a federal package that would override the patchwork of state-level AI regulation while pushing age-verification responsibility onto Apple and Google.

title: Wear OS 7 is here and your Pixel Watch is about to get a lot smarter
blurb: Google released Wear OS 7 to eligible Pixel Watch devices, adding Live Updates that mirror real-time phone notifications like delivery tracking and sports scores to the wrist, plus a media output switcher and up to 10% better battery life. Select watches will gain Gemini Intelligence features later this summer, including plain-language widget creation, multi-step app automation, and personalized suggestions pulled from Gmail and Search history.

title: Cancelled Xbox 360 version of GoldenEye 007 gets recompiled for PC — ‘No emulator, the game runs as a real native executable,’ insists dev
blurb: A developer statically recompiled the cancelled Xbox 360 version of GoldenEye 007 into a native Windows port with online multiplayer and 60 FPS.

title: Microsoft debuts Surface Pro and Surface Laptop with new jade green color and  Qualcomm Snapdragon X2 chips — refreshed devices start at $1,499 with 16GB of RAM
blurb: Microsoft refreshed its consumer Surface Pro and Surface Laptop lines with Qualcomm's Snapdragon X2 Plus and Elite chips, starting at $1,499 with 16GB of RAM. The update arrived via blog post rather than a dedicated event, adding a jade green color option, a new haptic touchpad on the Laptop, and up to 20 hours of battery life.

title: Cache to the Future: A Distributed Webpage Archive for Internet Blackouts
blurb: Researchers introduced Cache to the Future (CttF), a distributed system that caches and delivers static web content during internet blackouts caused by outages or government action. CttF crowdsources caching through community ratings while using digital signatures and proofs-of-work to block adversarial tampering, and city-scale simulations showed it delivering content across both normal and hostile conditions.

title: HSBC Expects Over $100 Million Gains From Using Google AI Tools
blurb: HSBC will use Google Cloud to deploy AI across its global operations, with individual projects each expected to generate over $100 million in added revenue or cost savings. The bank framed the rollout as a bet that AI can move beyond pilots into measurable financial returns, deepening Google's push into enterprise banking.

title: 10Gb/s Ethernet: switching to a Broadcom SFP+ module
blurb: Swapping a hot-running Marvell 10GBASE-T SFP+ module for a Broadcom-based 10Gtek one to stop thermal flapping on a home 10Gb/s LAN.

title: Making espresso with ultrasound
blurb: Researchers at UNSW Sydney developed an ultrasonic brewing method that produces espresso-strength coffee using room-temperature water, cutting energy use by up to 75%. Published in the Journal of Food Engineering, the process uses high-frequency sound waves to extract flavor and caffeine in under three minutes, and 100 blind taste-testers could not distinguish it from traditionally brewed espresso.

title: A Wearable Multimodal Ultrasound+Inertial System for Real-Time Virtual Reality Interaction
blurb: Researchers built a fully wearable VR interface that maps muscle activity to controls using A-mode ultrasound plus accelerometry sensors on the forearm and upper arm, eliminating the external cameras most systems require. Across five subjects, it hit 92% to 96% success rates on grasping, pinching, and pouring tasks after five minutes of fine-tuning, drawing just 19.9 mW for over 2.5 days of continuous use on a 350 mAh battery.

title: SpaceX Drew $800 Million From Korean Investors on Trading Debut
blurb: South Korean retail investors bought nearly $800M of SpaceX shares on the stock's first day of trading. The demand followed their exclusion from the world's largest IPO, underscoring strong local appetite for exposure to Elon Musk's rocket and satellite company.

title: Anthropic’s latest feud with the Trump admin may actually help it, sales data suggests
blurb: Ramp data shows Anthropic surpassed OpenAI in business AI spending for the first time in May, capping a month that also included a $65B raise at a $965B valuation and a confidential IPO filing. The Trump administration then ordered Anthropic to block non-Americans from its Mythos 5 and Fable 5 models, forcing their withdrawal, though Ramp's lead economist expects the "too dangerous to use" label to boost adoption rather than hurt it.

title: Dealogic: investors have poured $58B across 42 data center deals YTD; Oxford Economics says ~850 data centers worth some $7T are under construction globally (Financial Times)
blurb: Investors poured $58B into 42 data center deals so far this year, according to Dealogic. Oxford Economics estimates roughly 850 data centers worth about $7T are under construction worldwide, underscoring how capital is racing to build out AI compute capacity faster than demand has been proven.

title: A profile of Anthropic researcher Nicholas Carlini, who warned about Mythos in March and is now part of an Anthropic team briefing the White House on safeguards (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: The Wall Street Journal profiles Anthropic researcher Nicholas Carlini, who flagged the Mythos threat in March and now sits on a company team briefing the White House on AI safeguards. The piece highlights how Anthropic is positioning its security researchers as direct advisers to government, deepening the company's role in shaping federal policy on AI risk.

title: Brookfield-Backed Data Center Firm Csquare Files for US IPO
blurb: Csquare Inc., a data center operator backed by Brookfield Corp., filed publicly for a US IPO. The debut adds to a wave of public listings tied to AI infrastructure, as investors race to fund the compute and power capacity behind the buildout.

title: Google Messages could soon get a sniffer tool to help you spot AI-generated photos in chats
blurb: Google is building C2PA Content Credentials detection into Messages, letting users check whether photos shared in a chat were created or edited with AI, according to code strings found in a recent beta. Unlike Google's proprietary SynthID, C2PA is an open standard backed by Adobe, Microsoft, and Qualcomm, and would surface granular labels like "Parts of this media were made with AI" rather than a single yes-or-no verdict.

title: SpaceX's blistering start still faces key tests that will determine the stock's true value
blurb: SpaceX closed its third trading day at $201.68, briefly carrying a market cap above Amazon's $2.65T before settling roughly even. The early surge masks thin price discovery: the company set its own $135 IPO price, generates little revenue, and rests its trillion-dollar projections on unproven bets like Starship reusability and orbital data centers, leaving its true valuation unresolved for months.

title: A Czech AI startup says it can detect drones by sound for €150 per sensor, and it wants to wire up power grids first
blurb: Czech startup Neuron Soundware built Sound Shield, an AI system that detects drones by their engine sound using €100-150 sensors that draw just 1W each and run neural networks on Nvidia Jetson modules. The company is pitching dual-use deployment on electrical transformer stations, where the microphones would monitor equipment health while also listening for low-flying drones that radar-absorbing coatings can hide but cannot silence.

title: Lawmakers leary about Trump administration’s Anthropic order
blurb: Members of Congress responded with skepticism Tuesday to the Trump administration's order imposing export controls on Anthropic's newest AI models, which the company said forced it to disable its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 systems. Several lawmakers questioned whether the move stemmed from legitimate national security concerns or the administration's ongoing feud with Anthropic over domestic surveillance and autonomous weapons.

title: Threads reaches 500 million user milestone
blurb: Threads reached 500 million monthly active users, adding new community features and algorithm controls as it closes in on the active-user count of Elon Musk's X. The Meta-owned app has grown quickly since its 2023 launch, leaning on Instagram integration to court users wary of X's direction under Musk.

title: Trump admin tries to block Clean Air Act lawsuit over xAI's gas turbines
blurb: The Trump administration's DOJ urged a federal judge to dismiss an NAACP Clean Air Act lawsuit against Elon Musk's xAI, arguing the company's gas turbines in Southaven, Mississippi power Grok systems the military needs. The suit alleges xAI ran up to 57 unpermitted turbines at its Colossus gas plant, while the DOJ filing claims Grok's Gov Model aided targeted strikes in Iran during Operation Epic Fury.

title: The Hacker Sent by Anthropic to Calm the Government’s Nerves About AI Safety
blurb: Anthropic dispatched researcher Nicholas Carlini, a security expert who previously warned about AI's dangers, to reassure government officials that its latest models are safe to release. The move highlights how frontier labs are leaning on in-house safety credibility to ease regulatory and public concerns, as Carlini now helps make the case for shipping the very systems he once scrutinized.

title: Atom Computing raises $300M to build world’s first fault-tolerant, commercially viable quantum computer
blurb: Atom Computing raised $300M to accelerate its roadmap toward a commercially viable, fault-tolerant quantum computer, including a $100M Series C led by Third Point Ventures and a $100M commitment from the U.S. Department of Commerce. The company uses a neutral-atom approach rather than the superconducting qubits favored by Google and IBM, and recently became the second firm to publicly demonstrate quantum error correction.

title: Cockroaches scurry around with thousands of pieces of bacterial genomes
blurb: A new study found that cockroach genomes contain thousands of fragments of bacterial DNA acquired through horizontal gene transfer over millions of years. The finding shows that this gene-swapping, long considered common mainly among microbes, also occurs frequently in multicellular animals, where foreign DNA must reach the nucleus of germ cells to be inherited rather than just any cell.

title: Sharp’s first smartwatch can tell how many calories you’ve eaten without logging a single meal
blurb: Sharp's first smartwatch, the Karada Mate Watch, uses a bioelectrical impedance sensor and HEALBE's FLOW Technology to estimate calorie intake in real time by tracking body water and sugar shifts, no meal logging required. It launches in Japan on July 9 for 59,400 yen (~$370), pairing the OLED wearable with a $4-per-month app, though real-world accuracy will determine whether passive intake tracking finally works.

title: Qualcomm's new Snapdragon Reality Elite chip brings on-device AI to Android XR devices
blurb: Qualcomm announced the Snapdragon Reality Elite, a chip for premium mixed reality headsets and lightweight smart glasses that delivers up to 48 TOPS, enough to run large language and vision models directly on-device for the first time. The platform pushes 4.4K-per-eye visuals at 90fps with 160% higher NPU performance, 20% longer battery life, and 12 degrees Celsius cooler operation, powering Android XR devices launching later this year.

title: Sources: Chinese social media app Xiaohongshu targets a Hong Kong IPO as early as year-end at a $70B+ valuation, up from $50B in recent private secondary trades (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Xiaohongshu is targeting a Hong Kong IPO as early as year-end at a valuation above $70B, according to sources cited by the Wall Street Journal. The Chinese social media app's target marks a jump from the roughly $50B it commanded in recent private secondary trades, reflecting investor appetite for its growing advertising and e-commerce business.

title: Singapore’s Electronics Exports Jump by Record on AI Demand Boom
blurb: Singapore's electronics exports rose by the most on record last month, driven by surging global demand for AI-related equipment and components. The jump underscores how the AI buildout is rippling through Asia's electronics supply chain, lifting the export economies that manufacture and route the chips, servers, and hardware behind it.

title: Kazakhstan signs accords with Firebird on Nvidia-related computing projects that could bring up to $10B in investment, as it seeks to build a Data Center Valley (Bloomberg)
blurb: Kazakhstan signed accords with Firebird on Nvidia-related computing projects that could draw up to $10B in investment. The deals anchor the country's plan to build a "Data Center Valley," positioning Central Asia as a regional hub for AI compute as governments compete to host GPU capacity.

title: Snap opens pre-orders for Specs AR glasses
blurb: Snap opened pre-orders for Specs, its $2,195 augmented-reality glasses available in two sizes and loaded with hundreds of custom-built Snap Lenses. The launch puts Snap in direct competition with Apple and Meta for the consumer AR market, where both rivals carry far deeper hardware budgets and distribution.

title: Stop Killing Games fails to secure EU law despite 1.3M signatures
blurb: The European Commission declined to require publishers to keep discontinued games playable, rejecting the Stop Killing Games initiative despite 1.3M verified signatures.

title: A Red-Team Study of Anthropic Fable 5 & Opus 4.8 Models
blurb: This paper red-teams Anthropic's Fable 5 and Opus 4.8 against four families of automated jailbreak attacks across 7,826 harmful intents, with every apparent success re-adjudicated by a three-judge panel. Both models resist most attacks, but tree-of-attacks adaptive search broke Opus 4.8 on 11.5% of intents and Fable 5 on 6.1%, yielding 1,620 and 702 panel-confirmed harmful completions located cheaply with no human expert in the loop.

title: Looped World Models
blurb: LoopWM is the first looped architecture for world modeling, iteratively refining latent environment states through a parameter-shared transformer block. The approach reaches up to 100x parameter efficiency over conventional models and uses adaptive computation that scales depth to each prediction step's complexity, positioning iterative latent depth as a new scaling axis for world simulation.

title: Building Reliable Agentic AI Systems
blurb: Bayer and Thoughtworks built PRINCE, a cloud platform that uses agentic RAG and text-to-SQL to make decades of preclinical safety study reports searchable in natural language. The case study details two engineering pillars: context engineering, which shapes and routes information between specialized agents, and harness engineering, which builds orchestration, recovery, and observability around the models. Trust comes from transparency, explainability, and human-in-the-loop review.

title: Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization: Teacher in Prompts, Not Gradients
blurb: This paper introduces Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization (ZPPO), a distillation method that keeps the teacher inside the prompt rather than the policy gradient, avoiding the generalization loss and on-policy drift that plague logit imitation and teacher-injected RL. Tested on the Qwen3.5 family at four scales (0.8B-9B) with a 27B teacher across a 31-benchmark suite, ZPPO beat off/on-policy distillation and GRPO, with the largest gains at the smallest scale.

title: Running local models is good now
blurb: Local models have become genuinely useful, according to a developer running them on a 2022 M2 Mac with 64GB of RAM. The author marks GPT-OSS as the turning point where double-checking against frontier APIs became rare, and reports that Gemma 4 now enables agentic coding loops locally at roughly 75% the accuracy and speed of frontier models. Real tasks completed include refactoring a notebook into a multi-module repo, writing unit tests, and bootstrapping a two-tower recommendation model.

title: SpaceX to acquire AI coding platform Cursor for $60 billion
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire AI coding platform Cursor for $60 billion in an all-stock deal expected to close in Q3, two days after SpaceX's IPO and months after its merger with xAI. Cursor's revenue grew over the past year but its market share slipped as Anthropic's Claude Code took the lead, and the company had been bottlenecked on compute before tapping xAI's infrastructure.

title: Elon Musk Is Unleashing SpaceX’s New War Chest to Solve His AI Problem
blurb: Elon Musk is using SpaceX's expanded cash reserves to fund his AI ambitions, including a $60B acquisition of coding agent Cursor and a push into renting out data-center capacity. The moves are aimed at landing more enterprise customers and giving Musk's AI operations a stronger commercial footing against rivals like OpenAI and Anthropic.

title: Catastrophic Forgetting is Low-Rank: A Function-Space Theory for Continual Adaptation
blurb: This paper offers a function-space theory of catastrophic forgetting in the NTK regime, deriving a closed-form predictor for old-task prediction drift before any new-task gradient step is taken. The authors show that forgetting concentrates in a small number of old-task NTK eigenmodes, explaining why parameter-space regularizers miss output-space interference and motivating a targeted spectral regularizer.

title: Structural Role Injection in Handlebars-Templated LLM Prompts: Triple-Brace Interpolation, Delimiter Family, and the Limits of HTML Auto-Escaping
blurb: This paper shows that Handlebars, the default prompt-template engine in Microsoft Semantic Kernel, only blocks structural role injection for delimiter schemes its HTML escaping happens to cover, neutralizing ChatML, Llama-3, and XML delimiters while leaving Llama-2, legacy Human:/Assistant:, and Markdown delimiters intact. Across 5,760 trials on four models, GPT-3.5 Turbo followed task-hijack instructions in 91% of escaped trials, while Claude Haiku 4.5 resisted almost entirely.

title: Agent Bricks: Data + AI Summit 2026
blurb: Databricks expanded Agent Bricks into a full developer agent platform at Data + AI Summit 2026, letting developers build agents with any model and harness while Databricks handles infrastructure like sandboxes, memory, token capacity, deployment, and security. Since launch, over 100k agents have been built on the platform, now processing more than 1 quadrillion tokens per year for customers including AstraZeneca, 7-Eleven, Fox Corporation, and Block.

title: Feds freaked over Fable 5 after simple 'fix this code' prompt, not jailbreak, says researcher
blurb: A security researcher said federal officials raised alarms over the Fable 5 model after a simple "fix this code" prompt, not an actual jailbreak. The claim, attributed to the one person who reportedly read the underlying research paper, suggests the government response was triggered by routine model behavior rather than any deliberate exploit.

title: Ternary Mamba: Grouped Quantization-Aware Training of W1.58A16 State Space Models
blurb: Ternary Mamba shows that a pretrained Mamba-2 1.3B checkpoint can be compressed to ternary weights using grouped quantization-aware training and knowledge distillation from a frozen FP16 teacher, cutting the token budget 1,000x versus from-scratch methods. The approach shrinks the model 3.61x to 744 MB and reaches 48.1% zero-shot accuracy across seven tasks in just 102M tokens and four H100 GPU-hours.

title: Dynamic Rollout Editing for Reducing Overthinking in RL-Trained Reasoning Models
blurb: This paper introduces Dynamic Rollout Editing (DRE), a training-time method that reduces overthinking in RL-trained reasoning models by reframing it as a GRPO credit-assignment problem rather than a decoding-time stopping issue.
