The move folds a rocket company's balance sheet into the AI race, using SpaceX's reserves to buy distribution and compute rather than build them from scratch. For founders selling AI products, it signals that the deepest-pocketed players will increasingly acquire their way into the stack, turning today's standalone tools into someone else's enterprise on-ramp.

title: Databricks says annualized revenue rose 80%+ YoY to $6.9B, up from $5.4B in Q4; CEO Ali Ghodsi says higher AI agent usage is increasing costs, lowering margins (Jordan Novet/CNBC)
blurb: Databricks said annualized revenue rose more than 80% YoY to $6.9B, up from $5.4B in Q4, but CEO Ali Ghodsi flagged that heavier AI agent usage is pushing costs up and margins down. The growth comes from customers running more inference-hungry agentic workloads, the same demand that is eroding the unit economics underneath it. For founders building on top of this stack, it is a reminder that agent adoption scales compute spend faster than it scales margin, and pricing has to account for that.

title: Riding High After I.P.O., SpaceX Will Buy A.I. Start-Up for $60 Billion
blurb: SpaceX agreed to buy AI coding startup Cursor in an all-stock deal worth about $60B, days after its own blockbuster IPO. The move folds one of the fastest-growing developer tools into Elon Musk's AI push, giving him a frontier coding asset to pair with xAI. For founders, it marks how quickly post-IPO compute and equity get redeployed into AI consolidation, and how the buyers for breakout AI companies are increasingly platform giants rather than the labs that supply the models.

title: Anthropic, Trump Officials Seek Deal on Restoring Powerful Model Access
blurb: Anthropic and Trump administration officials are negotiating to restore access to the company's most powerful models, days after Anthropic pulled Fable and Mythos. In its first public comment since the move, the company said both sides are working quickly to resolve the dispute. For founders building on frontier APIs, the episode is a reminder that model access now sits inside a political relationship, and a supplier's regulatory standing can pull capabilities out from under your product overnight.

title: Exclusive: OpenAI Losses Increased Nearly 8X in 2025, With Spending Hitting $34 Billion
blurb: OpenAI's losses jumped nearly 8x in 2025, with the company burning $20.92B from operations on $13.07B in revenue against $34B in costs. R&D alone hit $19.18B, more than its entire top line, and the for-profit conversion added a $41.55B non-cash charge that pushed the net loss attributable to the company to $38.53B. Revenue more than tripled from $3.7B, but spending is scaling faster than sales, and the gap between what OpenAI takes in and what it spends to stay at the frontier is widening, not closing.

title: Intel begins production of most-advanced chip, inching closer to possible Apple deal
blurb: Intel began risk production of 18A-P, its most advanced node, which delivers 9% higher performance or 18% lower power than the 18A it has been making in Arizona since December. The stock is up over 200% this year on a turnaround bet that hangs on yield, and analysts say above-90% in the first month is what wins outside customers. The signal founders should watch is a credible second source to TSMC at the leading edge, though the Arm-versus-x86 gap still stands between Intel and an Apple deal.

title: Sources: a new AI lab founded by Junyang Lin, former lead of Alibaba's Qwen models, has raised several hundred million dollars at a ~$2B post-money valuation (The Information)
blurb: Junyang Lin, who led Alibaba's Qwen models, has left to start his own AI lab, raising several hundred million dollars at a roughly $2B post-money valuation. The round prices a months-old company with no shipped product almost entirely on the founder's track record building one of the strongest open-weight model families. Top model talent is now commanding venture rounds that once required real traction, and the labs that trained them are losing the people who define their roadmaps.

title: Qualcomm launches Snapdragon Reality Elite and a white-label toolkit for AI glasses, betting the next platform is not a phone
blurb: Qualcomm released Snapdragon Reality Elite, a mixed reality chip with 160% higher NPU performance, and START, a white-label toolkit that hands eyewear makers a near-complete smart glasses design they can brand and ship without building the stack. CEO Cristiano Amon told CNBC the company is working on more than 40 AI wearable form factors, from camera earbuds to pins to jewelry. The bet is that Qualcomm becomes the silicon supplier for whatever displaces the phone, the same position it held through the smartphone era, while letting hundreds of brands run the form-factor experiments.

title: SpaceX valuation balloons to $2.6T, briefly passes Amazon
blurb: SpaceX briefly passed Amazon to become the fifth-most valuable company on Tuesday, touching a $2.9 trillion valuation before settling near $2.6 trillion. The company lost $4.9 billion on $18.7 billion in revenue last year, while Amazon turned a $78 billion profit on $717 billion in sales, so the gap is a bet on the AI business Musk promises rather than the numbers on the page. SpaceX floated only 4% of its shares, and more than 300 million traded Tuesday, which is why a company can add roughly $1 trillion in value in three days.

title: Anthropic's Fable shutdown is a big moment for open-source AI
blurb: Anthropic abruptly pulled its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models for every customer to comply with a US export control directive, proving that access to a vendor's frontier model can be switched off overnight. The lesson operators took away is that a downloadable model running on your own infrastructure can't be cut off by a political fight, and the market noticed: Chinese open-source labs MiniMax and Zhipu both surged on the news. The catch is that the open models winning the most adoption right now are Chinese, so the hedge against vendor lock-in is itself becoming a dependency.

title: Sources: Jeff Bezos is investing in Cambridge, UK-based CuspAI, which applies generative AI to material sciences, as part of a $400M round at a $2.6B valuation (Tim Bradshaw/Financial Times)
blurb: Jeff Bezos is backing CuspAI, a Cambridge, UK startup applying generative AI to materials science, in a $400M round that values it at $2.6B. The bet is that AI can compress the discovery cycle for new materials, from carbon capture sorbents to battery chemistries, that traditionally took years of lab trial and error. Capital is flowing toward AI applied to physical science, not just software, and the names writing the checks now include the people who built the largest tech companies of the last era.

title: Sources: Amazon faces a possible FTC lawsuit alleging it misled advertisers; multiple state AGs are involved and the case could seek billions in civil penalties (Bloomberg)
blurb: The FTC is reportedly preparing a lawsuit accusing Amazon of misleading advertisers, with multiple state attorneys general joined to the case and civil penalties that could run into the billions. For founders, the signal is that ad-platform measurement and disclosure practices are now a regulatory target, not just a trust issue. If your growth depends on a few large ad platforms, the risk isn't only rising costs but sudden changes to how those platforms report and price what you buy.

title: 'A signal of where power sits': Trump and world leaders joined by OpenAI, Anthropic, Google at G7
blurb: Frontier AI CEOs including OpenAI's Sam Altman, Anthropic's Dario Amodei, and Google DeepMind's Demis Hassabis joined Trump and other G7 leaders at the Evian summit, a sign that heads of state now need the builders of the technology to make credible commitments on it. The meeting comes as Washington's export controls on Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models sit unresolved, with the release of Mythos pushing the administration toward regulation. Power is shifting toward the handful of executives actually shipping frontier models, and governments are now negotiating with them rather than around them.

title: Y Combinator reveals a new AI agent that can build and run an entire business just from a text message
blurb: Y Combinator launched Locus Founder, an AI agent that builds and runs an internet business from a single text. Send an idea over iMessage, SMS, or Telegram, and it handles market research across 40-plus paid data APIs, brand identity, full-stack site deployment, product sourcing, and outbound marketing. It also moves money, settling sales in USDC through a non-custodial wallet with spending controls and audit trails, so the agent can earn, hold, and spend under human oversight.

title: Series D funding rises 308% in first half of 2026
blurb: European Series D funding climbed 308% in the first half of 2026, the clearest sign yet that late-stage capital is flowing back after a long drought. The recovery is concentrated at the top of the stack, where investors are again willing to write nine-figure checks into proven companies rather than spread smaller bets across early rounds. For founders sitting on Series C cash, the implication is that the late-stage market has reopened, and the companies raising now are the ones with the metrics to justify the round.

title: Sources: the US has held off adding DeepSeek, CXMT, and 100+ other flagged companies to its Entity List, last updated in October, the longest gap in 10+ years (Karen Freifeld/Reuters)
blurb: The US has gone without updating its export-control Entity List since October, the longest gap in more than a decade, leaving DeepSeek, memory maker CXMT, and over 100 other flagged Chinese companies off the restricted roster. The delay keeps US suppliers free to sell to firms Washington has already identified as risks, and it signals that chip policy is now a bargaining chip in the broader trade negotiation. For founders building on or around China-exposed hardware and AI supply chains, the rules are frozen but not settled, and the next list could move fast.

title: China’s DeepSeek reportedly raises $7.4B in funding at $50B+ valuation
blurb: DeepSeek raised $7.4B at a $50B+ valuation, making it China's most valuable AI startup, with founder Liang Wenfeng putting in $3B and Tencent reportedly weighing $1.48B. The lab that crashed Nvidia's market cap 15% in early 2025 is now funded to keep pushing the same wedge: frontier-class quality at a fraction of the hardware, with V4-Pro processing million-token prompts on a KV cache one-tenth the size of its predecessor. Microsoft is reportedly eyeing a customized DeepSeek model for Cowork Copilot as a cheaper alternative to OpenAI and Anthropic, so the cost gap is starting to move real enterprise spend.

title: Plaud says its software business topped $100M in ARR after shipping over 2M AI notetakers
blurb: Plaud says its AI notetaker software business has crossed $100M in ARR after shipping more than 2M screenless devices, a rare proof point in a hardware category short on success stories. The economics come from hardware pulling subscriptions, with nearly half of device owners upgrading from the free 300-minute tier to paid pro or unlimited plans. The model is worth watching because Plaud doesn't sell standalone software, so each device sale is the top of a subscription funnel, even as Anker, Viaim, Vibe, and Pocket crowd the same market.

title: People prefer products with fewer ingredients
blurb: Most "organic" and "natural" claims now blur together because everyone uses them, but a simpler signal cuts through: telling buyers your product has "just a few" ingredients rather than "many" makes them up to 22% more likely to choose it. The mechanism is perceived purity, not actual ingredient count, so the win is in the framing of what you already have. If your product genuinely runs lean on ingredients, say so explicitly in the copy instead of leaning on the crowded organic and natural language.

title: How Roku fits into Fox's future — and what investors are missing about the deal
blurb: Fox is paying $22B for Roku, and its own shareholders hated it, sending the stock down 16% on Monday to a 52-week low and another 4% the next day. The market is pricing the new debt and the near-term cost, but the analysts who like the deal point to what investors are skipping: Fox isn't buying more content, it's buying the distribution platform, the first-party data, and the ad stack that turn its sports rights into a self-owned channel. The pattern for founders is the same one operators keep relearning, that the durable moat sits in the platform and the data, not the catalog.

title: Qualcomm wants to be the chip inside whatever replaces your smartphone, and it just announced two products toward that end
blurb: Qualcomm is betting the next computing platform isn't a phone, and CEO Cristiano Amon says the company is now working on more than 40 AI wearables spanning jewelry, camera earbuds, pins, and watches. It backed that bet with two products: Snapdragon Reality Elite, a mixed-reality glasses platform that runs a 3B-parameter model at 45 tokens per second, and START, a white-label toolkit of chips, software, and reference designs that lets eyewear makers like Inspecs and O'Neill ship AI glasses faster. The chipmaker wants to own the silicon layer before anyone agrees on the form factor.

title: Opinion | Why Does Trump Hate Anthropic?
blurb: The author argues that the Trump administration's de facto ban on Anthropic's Fable 5 model sets a dangerous precedent for how Washington treats domestic AI labs. The piece frames the move as the government picking winners and losers among American developers, citing Anthropic's friction with the White House over China policy and Pentagon contracts. The takeaway for founders is that political alignment is becoming a real variable in AI distribution, and a model's market access can now hinge on who controls it rather than how it performs.

title: General Atlantic in Talks to Lead Round for China’s Kling AI
blurb: Kuaishou's video-AI arm Kling is in talks to raise about $2B at an $18B valuation, with General Atlantic lined up to lead the round ahead of a planned IPO. The move is as much about optics as capital, since landing a marquee US investor signals legitimacy to Western markets at a moment when Chinese AI startups face heavy scrutiny on access to capital. General Atlantic's willingness to anchor the round shows that the appetite for frontier video models is strong enough to override the geopolitical friction that has frozen much of US-China tech investment.

title: Dealogic: investors have poured $58B across 42 data center deals YTD; Oxford Economics says ~850 data centers worth some $7T are under construction globally (Financial Times)
blurb: Capital is pouring into data center buildout at a scale that dwarfs prior infrastructure cycles. Dealogic counts $58B across 42 data center deals so far this year, while Oxford Economics tallies roughly 850 facilities worth about $7T under construction globally. The bet is that AI demand absorbs all of it, but that much committed capital sets a high bar for utilization, and any shortfall in usage lands as stranded compute and pressured returns.

title: China Watchdog Urges More IPOs From AI, Hong Kong Listed Firms
blurb: China's top securities regulator pledged to welcome more domestic IPOs from AI developers and Hong Kong-listed firms, aiming to deepen onshore capital markets. The move opens a state-backed funding path for Chinese AI startups at a moment when the US IPO window stays narrow and venture exits are scarce. For founders, the divergence is the signal: Beijing is engineering liquidity for its AI sector through public markets while Western unicorns sit on paper wealth with nowhere to list.

title: Judge Dismisses Elon Musk’s xAI Trade-Secret Lawsuit Against OpenAI
blurb: A judge dismissed Elon Musk's xAI trade-secret suit against OpenAI, which alleged OpenAI poached an engineer who worked on its chatbot.

title: Probably raises $9M to build a more reliable kind of AI
blurb: Probably raised $9M from a16z to catch LLM hallucinations with a deterministic validator harness that lets smaller, cheaper models hit 99.99% accuracy.

title: SpaceX Drew $800 Million From Korean Investors on Trading Debut
blurb: South Korean retail investors bought almost $800M of SpaceX shares on the stock's first trading day, after being locked out of the company's record-setting IPO. The scramble shows how much demand for marquee private names has built up behind closed markets, and how that pent-up appetite reroutes into any opening it can find. For founders, it is a reminder that the deepest pools of capital chasing your category may sit well outside the usual venture and US-retail channels.

title: Brookfield-Backed Data Center Firm Csquare Files for US IPO
blurb: Csquare, the Brookfield-backed data center operator, filed publicly for a US IPO, joining a wave of debuts tied to AI infrastructure. The listing tests whether public markets will keep funding the compute buildout that frontier AI now depends on, after a year in which private capital carried most of the capex. For founders, it signals that data center economics are moving from venture-style bets to public-market scrutiny, where revenue durability and power contracts matter more than the AI narrative.

title: Dear SaaStr:  My Top Sales Rep Made $600,000 a Year — And Just Quit!  What Happened?
blurb: Your top sales rep usually quits because of change, not money. A new VP of Sales arrives, the old one who sheltered them leaves, or next year's number simply looks harder to hit, and they bolt even when it means starting over for less. The rep thrived in the current environment and rarely reads change as good for them. You can't fully control it, but you can align any incoming VP with your top AE and pull that rep into the hiring process before the new boss lands.

title: When deep research isn't enough for your business: Sakana AI launches 'ultra deep research' agent for 100+ page reports in 8 hours
blurb: Sakana AI launched Marlin, a B2B "Virtual CSO" agent that runs eight-hour reasoning loops to produce cited 100-page strategy reports.

title: SpaceX's blistering start still faces key tests that will determine the stock's true value
blurb: SpaceX's IPO is red hot, with the stock briefly carrying a market cap above Amazon's $2.65 trillion on its third day before closing at $201.68. The problem is price discovery, because the company named its own offering price at $135 and a few days of trading reveals demand during the honeymoon, not the real number. Underneath sits a business that makes very little money, burns enormous cash, and projects trillion-dollar addressable markets on unproven bets like orbital data centers, so the true valuation likely won't be clear for weeks to months.

title: Startup Backed by Ex-Google CEO Debuts Robot, LG Partnership
blurb: A startup backed by former Google CEO Eric Schmidt debuted an AI-powered industrial robot built in partnership with LG, as capital keeps flooding into humanoids. The robotics push is no longer about consumer demos but about putting machines on factory floors, where labor shortages and reshoring give the economics a clearer payback. Schmidt's name and an LG manufacturing partner signal where the smart money thinks the near-term value sits: industrial automation that ships, not household helpers that impress in a video.

title: Atom Computing raises $300M to build world’s first fault-tolerant, commercially viable quantum computer
blurb: Atom Computing raised $300M to accelerate its push toward a commercially viable, fault-tolerant quantum computer, with $100M from a Third Point Ventures-led Series C and another $100M committed by the U.S. Department of Commerce. The company is betting on neutral-atom qubits rather than the superconducting approach Google and IBM are chasing, and it recently became the second firm to publicly demonstrate quantum error correction after crossing 1,000 qubits. Government money sitting alongside venture capital signals that fault-tolerant quantum has moved from a research bet to a strategic one worth subsidizing.

title: Sources: Chinese social media app Xiaohongshu targets a Hong Kong IPO as early as year-end at a $70B+ valuation, up from $50B in recent private secondary trades (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Xiaohongshu is targeting a Hong Kong IPO as early as year-end at a valuation north of $70B, up from the roughly $50B it fetched in recent private secondary trades. The jump shows Chinese consumer tech regaining IPO appetite after a long freeze, with private markets repricing the company upward faster than public listings have opened back up. For founders watching exit windows, it signals that Hong Kong is reemerging as a viable path to liquidity for the region's largest private companies.

title: Wolfram Language and Mathematica Version 15, AI Assistant, Symbolic Music, More
blurb: Wolfram released Version 15 of Wolfram Language and Mathematica, adding built-in AI assistant access, symbolic music representation, and a large batch of new core functionality across the language. The release also targets AIs as first-class users alongside humans, exposing interfaces designed to let LLM systems call Wolfram's computational engine for precise, formalized results rather than natural-language guesses.

title: Ternary Mamba: Grouped Quantization-Aware Training of W1.58A16 State Space Models
blurb: Ternary QAT from a pretrained Mamba-2 checkpoint compresses it 3.61x using 1,000x fewer tokens than training from scratch.

title: Why is Meta destroying its engineering organization?
blurb: Gergely Orosz argues that Meta has dismantled its two-decade high-performance engineering culture in just a few weeks, recasting software engineering from a profit center into a disdained cost center through AI-mandated workflows and pressure on core engineers. He ties the shift to leadership treating infra teams as expendable, citing a recent high-profile outage and internal disarray as early evidence the demolition is already backfiring.

title: Structural Role Injection in Handlebars-Templated LLM Prompts: Triple-Brace Interpolation, Delimiter Family, and the Limits of HTML Auto-Escaping
blurb: Handlebars' double-brace escaping, the documented safe default in Microsoft Semantic Kernel, only neutralizes role delimiters built from angle brackets, leaving Llama-2 [INST], Human:/Assistant:, and Markdown ### injections fully intact. Across 5,760 trials, GPT-3.5 Turbo followed task-hijack instructions in 91% of escaped prompts while Claude Haiku 4.5 resisted almost entirely, showing escaping cannot substitute for structural separation of instruction and data.

title: All Smoke, No Alarm: Oracle Signals in Agent-Authored Test Code
blurb: A study of 86,156 test-file patches from 33,596 agent-authored PRs across five coding agents found that 80.2% contain weak or no explicit oracle signals, meaning the tests run code without actually verifying behavior. After adjusting for agent, PR size, and language, strong-oracle PRs were 28% more likely to merge, suggesting test-file counts overstate verification strength and quality gates should check for assertions, not just test presence.

title: Context engineering is the new analytics engineering skill: a practical guide for dbt users
blurb: The dbt blog argues that context engineering, the practice of structuring information so AI systems reason about a domain without hallucinating, is the same work analytics engineers already do when they write MetricFlow metric definitions, model descriptions, and schema contracts. The guide contrasts agents querying raw tables, which guess and are frequently wrong, against agents querying a governed dbt Semantic Layer, which return consistent, defensible answers.

title: ProvenanceGuard: Source-Aware Factuality Verification for MCP-Based LLM Agents
blurb: ProvenanceGuard is a source-aware verifier that decomposes MCP-agent answers into atomic claims, routes each to source-specific evidence, and flags "cross-source conflation" where a claim is true but attributed to the wrong tool or source. On 40 held-out medical-domain traces it hit block F1 0.802 and source accuracy 0.858, though exact source-plus-relation accuracy fell to 0.229 on a harder multi-source benchmark with semantically close sources.

title: Verifying the Rust Standard Library
blurb: A crowdsourced verification campaign integrated multiple formal tools into CI on a fork of the Rust standard library to statically prove the correctness of its unsafe code, which currently relies only on testing and dynamic Miri checks. The authors machine-checked proofs ruling out a subset of undefined behavior, including out-of-bounds access, null and dangling pointer dereferences, and use of uninitialized memory, and lay out the remaining gaps as open problems for the formal-methods community.

title: Cordon: Semantic Transactions for Tool-Using LLM Agents
blurb: Cordon is a transactional runtime that stages a tool-using agent's irreversible effects in shadow state and an effect outbox, validating the full multi-step execution flow before committing or releasing any external action. The system replaces per-call guardrails with a task-scoped boundary supporting commit, rollback, and audit, catching cross-step violations that existing defenses miss while adding modest approval and latency overhead.

title: Humiliating IIS servers for fun and jail time
blurb: This bug bounty walkthrough details a methodology for finding and exploiting IIS web servers, covering discovery via Shodan and Google dorks, fingerprinting through response headers like `Server: Microsoft-IIS/10.0`, and exploitation of common misconfigurations. The author also demonstrates bypassing WAFs using HTTP parameter pollution, treating the default IIS splash page as a signal of likely misconfiguration rather than a dead end.

title: Stanford's DeLM cuts multi-agent task costs 50% — without a central orchestrator
blurb: Stanford researchers released DeLM, a decentralized multi-agent framework that drops the central orchestrator in favor of a shared knowledge base where agents coordinate directly off one another's verified progress. The approach cuts task costs by roughly 50% by removing the merge-filter-rebroadcast bottleneck, letting agents build on prior findings and recover detailed evidence only when needed.

title: Microsoft AntiSSRF open-source library helps block server-side request forgery
blurb: Microsoft released AntiSSRF, an open-source MIT-licensed library for .NET and Node.js that validates URLs and outbound connections to block server-side request forgery before applications make untrusted requests. Configuration centers on an AntiSSRFPolicy object for allow/deny IP lists and HTTP rules, plus a URIValidator with built-in checks for Azure Key Vault and Storage domains, with samples for Axios, follow-redirects, and node-fetch.

title: [AINews] GLM-5.2: the top Frontend Coding model in the world, IndexShare for Speculative Decoding
blurb: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, an MIT-licensed open-weight model with a 1M-token context window and two reasoning-effort modes, that independent leaderboards rank just behind Opus 4.8 on coding and ahead of every Opus on frontend coding. The 744B-parameter model ships with day-zero support across Transformers, vLLM, and SGLang, and uses a tweaked DeepSeek Sparse Attention variant to hold efficiency at ultra-long contexts.

title: Wear OS 7 helps your smartwatch keep up with you
blurb: Wear OS 7 is rolling out to eligible Pixel Watch devices, adding Android Live Updates on the wrist, cross-device media and earbud controls, and system-level power optimizations that deliver up to 10% better battery life over Wear OS 6. Select devices get Gemini Intelligence later this year, including natural-language widget creation via Create My Widget and multi-step app automation that runs tasks directly from the watch.

title: Google Flight Simulator
blurb: Google added an experimental flight simulator to the web version of Google Earth, letting users fly over streamed 3D buildings and high-resolution imagery using keyboard controls. The mode runs in-browser only, uses simplified physics aimed at casual exploration rather than aerodynamic training, and pauses with an instant restart when the aircraft hits terrain.

title: How to Optimize Transformer-Based Models for Low-Precision Training
blurb: This NVIDIA guide shows how to translate a transformer config and batch size into the exact M×K×N GEMM shapes a model executes, then microbenchmark those shapes across BF16, FP8, and NVFP4 to predict where low precision actually pays off. Using a 5B-parameter CodonFM RNA model as the example, it leans on Transformer Engine for quantization and kernel dispatch so teams can estimate speedups on Hopper and Blackwell before committing to an expensive training run.

title: GameCraft-Bench: Can Agents Build Playable Games End-to-End in a Real Game Engine?
blurb: GameCraft-Bench tests whether coding agents can build playable Godot games end-to-end across 140 tasks; the strongest agent scores just 41.46%.

title: Correlated randomness in Slay the Spire 2
blurb: The author shows that Slay the Spire 2's separate pseudorandom number generators are correlated, so observing one early RNG output leaks information that predicts the others, producing oddities like a 54% chance that Neow's Bones yields the Debt curse. The root cause is initializing each generator from the same seed plus a per-purpose hash, which leaves their first outputs statistically linked rather than independent.

title: Android 17 starts hitting Pixel phones and watches today
blurb: Android 17 begins rolling out to Pixel phones and watches, adding a Bubbles system that opens any app as a floating window.

title: The Value of Adaptivity in LSM Bloom-Filter Tuning: A Log-Law and a Two-Clock Frontier
blurb: This paper analyzes when adaptive Bloom-filter tuning in LSM trees actually beats the static Monkey optimum, deriving a log-law that makes optimal bits-per-key affine in the logarithm of access frequency and showing that coarse hotness estimates lose little because the workload enters only logarithmically. On Twitter production traces and a real RocksDB engine, reallocating filters only at compaction captures 96-99% of continuous tracking's benefit, since compaction already rebuilds filters for free on its own clock.

title: Bifrost: Hybrid TEE-FHE Inference for Privacy-Preserving Transformer and LLM Serving
blurb: Bifrost splits LLM inference between a CPU TEE and FHE on the accelerator, cutting projected latency up to 9.9x while keeping prompts ciphertext-only.

title: ICYMI: OpenAI released CDP support for browser use on Codex
blurb: OpenAI added Chrome DevTools Protocol access to Codex, letting the agent profile JavaScript performance, read console output, network traffic, and rendered state, and rewrite a page's DOM directly from its embedded browser or Chrome. The opt-in developer mode is gated behind a Settings toggle, excluded from the EEA, UK, and Switzerland at launch, and still runs slowly and undertrained on the tooling, often needing several prompting attempts.

title: Android 17 arrives on Pixel phones today
blurb: Google began rolling out Android 17 to compatible Pixel phones today, with other manufacturers expected to ship the update through 2026. The release adds Bubbles for floating app windows with a dedicated bubble bar on foldables and tablets, native controller remapping across all Android phones, foldable gaming controls, and Pixel-exclusive additions including AirDrop support via Quick Share on the 8A and 9A.

title: Google launches 'bubble' multi-tasking and foldable gaming mode with Android 17
blurb: Android 17 is rolling out to Pixel devices today, adding Bubbles, which turns any app into a floating window via long-press so you can view two apps over a third, plus a foldable gaming mode with a 50/50 game-and-gamepad layout and native controller remapping. The release also ships Screen Reactions for simultaneous selfie-and-screen recording, temporary per-app location grants, and a biometric remote lock in Find Hub.

title: Linux 7.1 arrives with an NTFS overhaul and major hardware performance boosts
blurb: Linus Torvalds released Linux 7.1, a stable kernel headlined by a new NTFS driver, Intel FRED support for faster Panther Lake CPUs, quicker Intel Arc Battlemage graphics, and improved support for older AMD Radeon GPUs. The release also bundles USB heap-overflow fixes plus use-after-free, memory-leak, and refcount corrections across i2c, zram, gpio, and net, with the Linux 7.2 merge window now open.

title: PracRepair: LLM-Empowered Automated Program Repair Inspired by Human-Like Debugging Practices
blurb: PracRepair is an LLM-based automated program repair framework that builds on-demand static-dynamic context from failing executions, runs question-driven failure diagnosis to form explicit repair hypotheses, and refines patches using validation diagnostics and trace-level behavioral changes. On Defects4J V1.2/V2.0 it fixes 162/171 bugs under GPT-4o, beating state-of-the-art baselines and generalizing to real-world bugs across multiple models.

title: Start fresh, don't lift and shift: a dbt migration guide
blurb: This dbt migration guide argues that lift-and-shift migrations reproduce the old data model's problems in Jinja, leaving teams with one-to-one stored-procedure-to-model mappings, source-system names like salesforce_accounts_cleaned, no staging models, and hardcoded schemas instead of ref() lineage. It lays out six diagnostic symptoms of a legacy migration and argues teams should treat the move as a rewrite of the data model, not a mechanical port of existing code.

title: Firefox 152 understands “Sssh!”
blurb: Firefox 152 adds a Quick Action that silences every audio-playing tab across all windows when users type "mute," "sssh," or "hush" into the address bar. The release also ships experimental JPEG XL support, multi-tab sending and URL copying to other devices, a redesigned Settings layout, and roughly 40 security fixes.

title: Position: Coding Benchmarks Are Misaligned with Agentic Software Engineering
blurb: The authors argue that coding benchmarks built in the pre-agent era are misaligned with how agents actually work, since they collapse model, harness, environment, and feedback into one end-to-end score graded against a single reference solution. They identify three failures: scores conflate the model with the surrounding harness, valid alternative solutions get penalized, and the lack of component-level signal makes the system hard to iterate on.

title: PromptMN: Pseudo Prompting Language
blurb: PromptMN is a pseudo-prompting DSL that annotates prose with %-prefixed typed directives for roles, goals, constraints, and outputs.

title: Linux kernel 7.1 sends Intel 486 support to silicon heaven
blurb: The Linux kernel 7.1 release drops support for Intel's 80486 and early 586 processors, removing decades-old workarounds for CPUs that lack the CMPXCHG8B and TSC instructions modern x86 code assumes. The cleanup deletes legacy emulation paths from the kernel's boot and locking code, simplifying maintenance for the 32-bit x86 architecture that remains in embedded and retro-computing use.

title: Boot Naked Linux
blurb: The author boots a minimal Linux system with nothing but a kernel and a single statically linked program running as PID 1, skipping the usual initramfs, init system, and userspace entirely. The walkthrough covers compiling the program against the raw syscall interface, pointing the kernel at it via the init= boot parameter, and testing the result under QEMU before running on real hardware.

title: When the Next Step Is Not One Step: Distribution-Aware Execution Modeling for Concurrent Go Programs
blurb: This paper treats scheduler nondeterminism in concurrent Go programs as a training signal, running each program many times to build an empirical distribution of next events and fine-tuning a 7B model to match it with a KL objective. On 798 held-out predictions from real CockroachDB, Kubernetes, gRPC, and etcd bugs, it hits 36.2% accuracy and cuts Expected Calibration Error from 0.205 to 0.169 versus cross-entropy.

title: Google Chrome update will close the door on ad blockers
blurb: Google is removing the final Manifest V2 workaround in Chrome 150, expected June 30, fully ending support for MV2 extensions including ad blockers like uBlock Origin. The change deletes the `ExtensionManifestV2Disabled` flag as dead code, with remaining MV2 flags slated for Chrome 151 in July, and Chromium-based browsers like Edge and Opera expected to follow.

title: Cancelled Xbox 360 version of GoldenEye 007 gets recompiled for PC — ‘No emulator, the game runs as a real native executable,’ insists dev
blurb: GoldEye Recomp v1.0 statically recompiles the cancelled Xbox 360 GoldenEye 007 into a native C++ Windows executable, no emulator required.

title: SpaceX purchases Cursor, a Claude Code and OpenAI Codex competitor [U]
blurb: SpaceX acquired Cursor in a $60 billion deal, a week after the company went public and roughly two months after an initial $10 billion partnership granting Cursor access to xAI's Colossus infrastructure. The acquisition pulls a leading agentic coding tool, and a direct competitor to Anthropic's Claude Code and OpenAI's Codex, into SpaceX's expanding AI portfolio alongside xAI.

title: Understanding the rationale behind a rule when trying to circumvent it
blurb: Microsoft's "Old New Thing" argues that following a rule's letter without grasping its intent reproduces the exact failure the rule exists to prevent. The example is a driver whose process/thread callback correctly queues slow work to a System Worker Thread, then blocks waiting for it to finish, hanging the system because the real constraint was "return fast," not "delegate the work.

title: Agentic Code Review
blurb: Code review has become the most leveraged skill in software because coding agents now produce solid code faster than any human can read it, shifting the bottleneck from writing to deciding whether to trust a change. The author argues the right review approach depends entirely on context, and points the same agents at incoming PR queues to triage diffs, turning the tools generating the extra code into the means of keeping up with it.

title: I admire Fabrice Bellard. He is almost certainly a better overall programmer
blurb: John Carmack praised Fabrice Bellard, the prolific French engineer behind FFmpeg, QEMU, and QuickJS, as almost certainly a better overall programmer than himself.

title: Apple reveals why macOS might block your Terminal prompt
blurb: macOS 26.4 blocks Terminal pastes for infrequent users when commands are copied from websites, chats, or email to curb malware.

title: KDE Plasma 6.7 Released
blurb: KDE released Plasma 6.7, which finally ships per-screen virtual desktops after 21 years, along with an instant light/dark Global Theme toggle, per-app volume controls, and System Tray support for Flatpak "Background Apps". The release also matures printing with active-job badges, easier Windows shared-printer connections, and a new print queue management tool, plus Overview-screen desktop switching via scroll or Page Up/Down.

title: Building AI Agents for AR Glasses and XR Devices with NVIDIA XR AI
blurb: NVIDIA released XR AI in public beta, an open source library for building AI agents that run inside AR glasses and XR headsets by routing live camera, microphone, and data streams to GPU-accelerated models in the cloud, data center, or edge. The agents combine visual grounding via NVIDIA Cosmos, voice interaction with Nemotron models, and enterprise tool calls over MCP, with early use in field service, manufacturing, and lab research.

title: Group Commit Self-Clocks: Why Tuning Is Unnecessary Above a Device-Set Load Threshold
blurb: This paper shows that group commit's classic tuning knob (timer, batch size, or adaptive policy) is vacuous in closed-loop OLTP, where each client issues its next transaction only after the last commits. Modeling commits as a closed queueing network, the authors prove that a parameter-free greedy policy self-clocks to within about 0.1% of the best oracle-tuned timer above a device-set load threshold, and confirm on PostgreSQL that commit_delay=0 stays competitive.

title: Introducing OpenSharing: the Next Evolution of Delta Sharing for the Agentic Era
blurb: Databricks released OpenSharing, an open-source protocol that extends Delta Sharing beyond tables and files to models, agents, and AI skills across any cloud, vendor, or format, with native support for Delta Lake, Iceberg, and Parquet. The move builds on Delta Sharing's 28,000+ recipients and pushes zero-copy sharing into the agentic stack, letting Iceberg recipients and on-prem systems exchange semantic context and autonomous agents without vendor-locked formats.

title: Agent Bricks: Data + AI Summit 2026
blurb: Databricks expanded Agent Bricks into a full developer agent platform at Data + AI Summit 2026, after the original product hit 100k+ agents built and over 1 quadrillion tokens processed per year across customers like AstraZeneca, 7-Eleven, and Block. The pitch is that the agent loop is only 1% of the work, so Databricks now handles deployment, security, evaluation, memory, and token capacity across any model or harness, with newly added support for Kimi and a SpaceX partnership for Grok.

title: Databricks says annualized revenue rose 80%+ YoY to $6.9B, up from $5.4B in Q4; CEO Ali Ghodsi says higher AI agent usage is increasing costs, lowering margins (Jordan Novet/CNBC)
blurb: Databricks said annualized revenue rose more than 80% year-over-year to $6.9B, up from $5.4B in the prior quarter. CEO Ali Ghodsi noted that rising AI agent usage is driving up compute costs and compressing margins, a trade-off as customers shift more workloads to inference-heavy agentic workflows.

title: Databricks unveils CustomerLake, its agentic CDP
blurb: Databricks announced CustomerLake, an agentic CDP that runs natively on its lakehouse and is governed by Unity Catalog, consolidating identity resolution, audience building, campaign automation, and activation into one platform. The pitch is collapsing the gap between insight and action so the same models that generate customer context drive activation directly, rather than piping data out to disconnected martech tools, with Databricks claiming personalized experiences delivered 1 billion times a day.

title: What’s new with Unity Catalog at Data + AI Summit 2026
blurb: Databricks expanded Unity Catalog at Data + AI Summit 2026 with the Unity AI Gateway, which extends governance from data and AI assets to runtime interactions across models, agents, MCP servers, and tools. Other additions include a Glossary and Domains for shared business context, cross-cloud and cross-region addressability, and a Governance Hub command center, with more than 14,000 organizations now governing data and AI on the platform.

title: Announcing Apps on Databricks Marketplace
blurb: Databricks launched Apps on Databricks Marketplace in Public Preview, letting customers discover, install, and run third-party data and AI applications natively inside their Unity Catalog-governed workspaces with no data egress. Instead of moving data to an external tool, the app deploys to the data, and providers get a no-egress distribution channel to monetize proprietary apps across thousands of Databricks enterprises.

title: Introducing OpenSharing SecureConnect
blurb: Databricks released OpenSharing SecureConnect, a managed proxy that routes storage access for Delta Sharing recipients so providers allowlist Databricks endpoints once instead of configuring firewall rules per recipient. Data stays in the provider's own bucket with live sharing from the source, and providers can optionally use Network Connectivity Configuration for private link connectivity between the proxy and their storage.

title: The Stanford EDGAR Filings Dataset: Reconstructing U.S. Corporate and Financial Disclosures into Layout-Faithful and Token-Efficient Pretraining Data
blurb: Stanford researchers released the Stanford EDGAR Filings Dataset (SEFD), an open reconstruction of SEC filings into layout-faithful MultiMarkdown for long-context LLM pretraining. The corpus is token-efficient and has under 0.1% overlap with Common Crawl, addressing the growing scarcity of clean long-document training data. SEFD-v1 ships a 152B-token snapshot from a larger 550B-token archive, plus two benchmarks for filing-grounded numerical forecasting and complex financial-table transcription.

title: AI governance at Data + AI Summit 2026: What’s new with Unity AI Gateway
blurb: Databricks expanded Unity AI Gateway at Data + AI Summit 2026, extending Unity Catalog governance to models, agents, MCP services, and skills with runtime guardrails, unified tracing, and cross-provider cost controls including spend visibility, hard spend caps, and intelligent routing. The pitch is one governance layer for a multi-model, multi-agent estate, where teams cap token spend, attribute costs per agent, and monitor coding-agent activity without stitching together separate tools per provider.

title: Rethinking Dataset Distillation for Classification: Do Distilled Sets Outperform Coresets?
blurb: Dataset distillation has been pitched as beating coreset selection by synthesizing compact training sets instead of pruning to real samples, but this paper finds the opposite. Benchmarking seven SOTA distillation methods against three coreset strategies on ImageNet-1K, ImageNet100, and ImageNette under standardized protocols, the authors show distilled sets match or underperform coresets at large scale while costing far more to build. Coresets also gave better coverage of the original data distribution, making them the more efficient default for data-centric training.

title: Introducing new Explores and Merge Queries in Looker
blurb: Google added a suite of Gemini-powered AI assistants to Looker Explore, including an Insight Assistant that builds queries from natural language via the Conversational Analytics API and an Expression Assistant that writes custom calculations without requiring Looker Expression syntax. The update also ships AI-generated Explore summaries, auto-generated Quick Starts to remove the empty-canvas cold start, and a modernized UI with a resizable field picker and column-level contextual menus.

title: The Value of Adaptivity in LSM Bloom-Filter Tuning: A Log-Law and a Two-Clock Frontier
blurb: This paper asks when adaptive Bloom-filter tuning in LSM trees actually earns its complexity, building on Monkey's static optimum across synthetic sweeps, Twitter cache traces, and a real RocksDB engine. The authors find optimal bits-per-key is affine in the log of access frequency, so coarse hotness estimates lose little, and reallocating filters only at compaction captures 96-99% of continuous tracking's benefit. The takeaway is a three-regime policy that tells teams when fine-grained tracking is worth the machinery and when it isn't.

title: SNAS: A Multi-Layer Defense-in-Depth Architecture for Secure Egress in Sandboxed Workloads
blurb: Snowflake detailed SNAS, the production architecture that gives Snowpark's sandboxed UDFs external network access without breaking multi-tenant isolation. The design layers eBPF packet filtering, GENEVE overlay networks, and distributed egress proxies for policy-driven control, plus an EDT-based eBPF bandwidth limiter and safeguards against connection limits and port exhaustion. It runs across all Snowflake regions, supporting petabyte-scale transfers and latency-sensitive integrations to cloud APIs, external databases, and feature stores.

title: Databricks’ new agentic coworker Genie One brings AI automation to every part of the business
blurb: Databricks launched Genie One at its Data + AI Summit, an agentic coworker that extends the existing Genie suite from conversational analytics to executing tasks across sales, marketing, and finance workflows. The tool reasons over structured and unstructured data via Genie Ontology, a self-improving context layer that scans an org's documents, applications, and data to ground agents in business context rather than guessing to fill the gaps.

title: Group Commit Self-Clocks: Why Tuning Is Unnecessary Above a Device-Set Load Threshold
blurb: This paper argues that group commit's release-timer tuning is mostly unnecessary in real OLTP workloads. Because clients issue their next transaction only after the last commits, arrivals are closed-loop, so a parameter-free greedy policy that flushes the moment the log device is free self-clocks to a fixed point within ~0.1% of the best oracle-tuned timer. The square-root timer rule only matters below a device-set load threshold; on PostgreSQL, commit_delay=0 stays competitive with any tuned value.

title: Milvus invents Vector Lakebase
blurb: Zilliz launched Vector Lakebase, extending Milvus from real-time vector search into a unified S3-based foundation that runs production queries, discovery sessions, and multi-petabyte training pipelines against the same vectors without copies or migration. It stores data on Vortex, an open-source columnar format that claims roughly 100x faster random access and 10-20x faster scans than Parquet, with tiered serving for production and on-demand compute for batch analytics.

title: Introducing the Agentic CDP: A New Species of CDP for a New Era of Agents
blurb: Databricks introduced the "Agentic CDP," a customer data platform built for AI agents as first-class operators, with always-on 1:1 personalization, embedded governed customer context, and a "Golden Context" layer that extends the traditional Customer 360 record. The pitch targets agentic buying cycles that run in milliseconds, arguing that batch-oriented CDPs are too slow and too coarsely segmented to reach buyers whose purchasing agents filter out anything less than precisely relevant.

title: Collaborative Large and Small Language Models for Accurate and Scalable Data Repair
blurb: LasRepair pairs an LLM with a small language model for data repair, using the LLM as an instructor that selects a global repair context to guide a cheaper SLM corrector rather than repairing rows directly in their noisy raw context. Two extensions push accuracy further: an EM-style loop that alternates between updating the corrector and refining the context, and column-calibrated confidence weighting that down-weights uncertain rows. On real-world datasets, the full version beats the strongest baseline by 18.1% F1.

title: Snap finally debuts its long-awaited AR glasses, Specs, and, oof, they aren’t cheap
blurb: Snap released Specs, its first consumer AR glasses since 2019, priced at $2,195 with preorders open now and shipping this fall in the US, UK, and France. All computing runs on-device with no tether, two Snapdragon chips, and a 51-degree field of view, but at roughly 132 grams and well above Meta Ray-Bans' $350 floor, the design trades everyday wearability for standalone capability.

title: Wear OS 7 helps your smartwatch keep up with you
blurb: Google released Wear OS 7 to eligible Pixel Watch devices today, bringing Android Live Updates to the wrist for glanceable real-time tracking, cross-device media and earbud controls, and up to 10% better battery life over Wear OS 6. Select devices get Gemini Intelligence later this year, including Create My Widget for building custom dashboards through natural language and multi-step app automation that completes tasks directly from the watch.

title: Apple releasing 20th anniversary iPhone, AirPods with cameras next year: report
blurb: Apple is preparing a late-2027 lineup headlined by a 20th-anniversary iPhone with a nearly edge-to-edge display and curved glass wrapping the sides, plus a second-generation foldable and camera-equipped AirPods, all built on a 2nm A21 chip, according to a Bloomberg report from Mark Gurman. The AirPods, which use visual AI models to identify objects in a user's surroundings, slipped from 2026 to 2027 amid Apple's ongoing Siri and Apple Intelligence delays.

title: NBBJ wraps Vivo skyscraper in spiralling terrace
blurb: NBBJ completed Vivo's 150-metre Shenzhen headquarters in the Qianhai Bay business district, wrapping the 6,000-person tower in a terrace that spirals around its exterior. The continuous outdoor band breaks from the sealed-glass curtain-wall norm for Chinese corporate towers, treating accessible exterior space as a primary facade element rather than an amenity tacked onto the roof.

title: I tried the BBC's new 3D World Cup app, and football suddenly felt like a video game
blurb: The BBC's new 3D World Cup app uses Immersiv.io technology to turn FIFA's live player-tracking data into a navigable real-time 3D model of each match, letting viewers switch viewpoints, follow individual players, and zoom out to see team shape without a VR headset. The author frames it as interactive design built from raw match data, a second-screen experience that visualizes the tactical information most broadcasts keep hidden rather than replacing the live feed.

title: How Gaudí's Crypt paved the way for parametricism
blurb: Gaudí's hanging chain models for the Church of Colònia Güell, begun in 1898, used weighted strings and mirrors to derive structural form from gravity itself, computing catenary curves a century before software could. The technique prefigures parametric design's core idea: define the rules and constraints, then let the system generate the geometry rather than drawing it by hand.

title: Android 17 is about to make gaming on foldables way better
blurb: Android 17 introduces a foldable gaming mode that splits the unfolded display into a 50/50 layout, with the game on top and a dynamic gamepad filling the lower half. The update also adds native controller remapping and improved memory cleanup to cut frame drops, signaling Google's continued push to treat foldables as a serious gaming form factor.

title: The World Cup forced this brand to hide its logo – then it backfired
blurb: FIFA's "clean stadium" rules forced Levi's to cover its logo on San Francisco's Levi's Stadium with a white tarp, but the brand's unmistakable silhouette stayed recognizable and the cover-up drew more attention than the logo would have. Levi's leaned in, swapping its social profile pictures for the shrouded mark and posting a video welcoming the world to the "[redacted] stadium," turning forced compliance into free advertising.

title: Talking to Your Data: Exploring Embodied Conversation as an Interface for Personal Health Reflection
blurb: A research prototype replaces wearable-health dashboards with an embodied conversational agent that narrates your data as spoken statistics to prompt active reflection.

title: Snap's slimmed down AR Specs go on sale later this year for $2,195
blurb: Snap will sell its AR Specs to the public for the first time later this year at $2,195, with a slimmer 132-gram frame, a 51-degree field of view, and four hours of mixed-use battery. The glasses run a two-chip Snapdragon setup with contextual AI assistance from OpenAI and Google, and ship with a Lens Studio update that supports AI coding tools like Claude Code.

title: Temple built from six million reused tiles wins Brick Award 2026
blurb: Đạo Mẫu Temple and Museum in Vietnam won the grand prize at Wienerberger's 2026 Brick Awards, built from more than six million up-cycled clay tiles salvaged from 500 demolished houses. The win signals a growing institutional appetite for reuse-driven material strategies, treating salvaged brick and tile as a design language rather than a sustainability footnote.

title: Garden walls conceal compact London house by Inglis Badrashi Loddo
blurb: Inglis Badrashi Loddo completed Walled Courtyard, a single-storey 63-square-metre infill home hidden behind brick garden walls in Kennington, London.

title: No Liquid Glass slider for Apple Watch, but here’s how watchOS 27 design changes
blurb: Apple's Liquid Glass opacity slider, which lets iPhone, iPad, and Mac users dial the material between clear and frosted in iOS 27, is not coming to Apple Watch, where watchOS 27 keeps a single fixed setting for everyone. Watch users still get the broader Liquid Glass refresh seen across the iPhone, just with subtler translucency tweaks and no control over how much frosting appears.

title: How Taylor Swift, Lego and Star Wars nail brand collaboration
blurb: Strategic fit, authenticity, and a genuine cultural moment separate standout brand collaborations like Lego x Star Wars from forced ones.

title: How to Make AI Agents Follow Your Design System
blurb: The author argues that AI agents follow your codebase's actual patterns, not your rules file, because models weight real examples over prose and stop reading instructions buried in a long context window. The piece is a useful corrective for anyone whose AGENTS.md keeps growing: an agent that imports from your legacy folder and hardcodes #FF3B30 instead of semantic tokens is faithfully copying what your repo does, not ignoring what it says.

title: 2026 Core77 Design Awards Autodesk Fusion Prize - Otter Newborn Warmer by Design That Matters
blurb: Design That Matters won the 2026 Autodesk Fusion Prize for Otter, a portable conductive warmer treating newborn hypothermia in low-resource hospitals.

title: Microsoft just gave Outlook for Mac an ‘app-wide’ Liquid Glass update
blurb: Microsoft updated Outlook for Mac with an app-wide Liquid Glass refresh, refactoring the New button's motion, adding elevation and glass effects to profile controls, and widening corner radii to match Tahoe-native chrome. The release also adds PST import for migrating mail, calendars, and contacts, and targets parity with Apple's macOS 27 Golden Gate iteration of the design language.

title: iOS 27 adds new Liquid Glass slider on iPhone, here’s what it lets you do
blurb: iOS 27 replaces Liquid Glass's Clear/Tinted toggle with a slider in Settings, letting you dial transparency and tint to any level systemwide.

title: GhostTree Attack Abused Recursive Windows Junctions to Hide Malware
blurb: Researchers disclosed GhostTree, a Windows evasion technique that abuses NTFS junctions pointed at their own parent directory to create recursive loops generating effectively infinite file paths, causing recursive scanners and some EDR products to hang before they ever examine malware in the folder. Junction creation requires only write access and no admin privileges, so defenders should constrain directory recursion depth and flag reparse points that loop back on their own ancestors.

title: A Red-Team Study of Anthropic Fable 5 & Opus 4.8 Models
blurb: A red-team study using the HackAgent framework tested Anthropic's Fable 5 and Opus 4.8 against four jailbreak families across 7,826 harmful intents, with every apparent success re-adjudicated by a three-judge majority vote. Both models neutralize static obfuscation but remain vulnerable to adaptive iterative attacks: tree-of-attacks broke Opus 4.8 on 11.5% of intents and Fable 5 on 6.1% worst-case, yielding 1,620 and 702 panel-confirmed harmful completions. The authors conclude that even hardened frontier models stay reliably breakable under sustained automated pressure.

title: OptinMonster WordPress plugin hacked in CDN supply-chain attack
blurb: Attackers compromised Awesome Motive's CDN by exploiting a known UpdraftPlus flaw on a marketing server holding CDN credentials, then used the stolen API key to inject malicious JavaScript into OptinMonster, TrustPulse, and PushEngage plugin files served to over 1.2 million WordPress sites. The script ran when admins browsed infected pages, stealing auth tokens to create rogue admin accounts and install a self-hiding backdoor with a web shell and PHP execution.

title: A backdoor in a LinkedIn job offer
blurb: A developer documented receiving a fake LinkedIn recruiter offer that funneled them into a take-home coding challenge whose repo hid an obfuscated backdoor in a config file, designed to exfiltrate credentials and environment data when the project was run. The lure matches the DPRK-linked Contagious Interview campaign targeting engineers, so treat unsolicited coding assignments as untrusted code and run them only in isolated, network-restricted sandboxes.

title: Cybercriminals mask malicious communications through Microsoft Teams relays
blurb: DragonForce ransomware affiliates deployed Backdoor.Turn, malware that hides C2 traffic inside legitimate Microsoft Teams TURN relay infrastructure, marking what Symantec calls the first in-the-wild abuse of TURN this way. Defenders monitoring network traffic see only outbound connections to genuine Microsoft servers, while the attackers used DLL sideloading and BYOVD drivers to disable security tools and dwelled one to two months.

title: SimpleHelp RMM flaw could give attackers full access to managed endpoints (CVE-2026-48558)
blurb: CVE-2026-48558, a critical authentication-bypass in SimpleHelp RMM, lets unauthenticated remote attackers forge a "Technician" account on servers using OIDC authentication, then remote into managed endpoints and execute scripts, bypassing enforced MFA via first-login self-registration. SimpleHelp patched it in v5.5.16 and v6.0 RC 2 in late May; Horizon3.ai found roughly 7.2% of the nearly 14,000 internet-exposed servers run the vulnerable OIDC config, so patch and audit Technician accounts now.

title: Windows version of SprySOCKS Linux malware used to attack govt orgs
blurb: ESET discovered Windows variants of the SprySOCKS malware family, attributed with high confidence to Chinese threat actor Earth Lusca (FishMonger/Aquatic Panda/TAG-22), used against government organizations in Taiwan, Thailand, Pakistan, and Honduras during 2023 and 2024. The WIN_DRV variant loads a kernel driver signed with a leaked certificate to hide processes, files, network connections, and registry keys, and diverts arbitrary TCP traffic to the backdoor to avoid exposing a listening port.

title: SimpleHelp bug lets hackers create rogue remote support accounts
blurb: SimpleHelp patched CVE-2026-48558, a critical flaw that lets unauthenticated attackers create privileged Technician accounts on servers using OIDC authentication, bypassing MFA to remote into managed endpoints and execute scripts. It affects versions 5.5.15 and older where OIDC and group-authenticated logins are enabled; patch to 5.5.16 or 6.0RC2, or restrict technician logins via IP allowlists and hunt for unknown technician accounts in server.log.

title: New Rokarolla Android Malware Steals PINs, SMS Codes, and Crypto Wallet Funds
blurb: Zimperium's zLabs documented Rokarolla, a new Android banking trojan that targets 217 banking and cryptocurrency apps with 137 remote commands, lifting lock-screen PINs via fake-lockscreen overlays, intercepting SMS one-time codes, rewriting the clipboard to redirect crypto payments, and disabling Google Play Protect. It spreads through malicious sites impersonating apps like TikTok and Chrome, installing a dropper disguised as Play Protect that abuses Accessibility access and rotates through multiple fallback C2 domains.

title: Bug in FIFA World Cup internal system gave anyone ability to modify TV stream
blurb: A broken access control flaw in FIFA's back-end API let anyone who registered on its public player-agent platform reach internal World Cup systems, including the broadcast controls that dictate what TV viewers and on-air commentators see. Researcher BobDaHacker reported the missing authorization check on Tuesday and FIFA patched it within hours, though it could have allowed an attacker to hijack every match camera simultaneously.

title: CISA warns of another cPanel plugin flaw exploited in attacks
blurb: CISA added CVE-2026-48172, an actively exploited symlink-following flaw in the LiteSpeed cPanel user-end plugin, to its KEV catalog, giving FCEB agencies three days under BOD 26-04 to patch. The bug lets attackers with FTP or web shell access escalate to root on shared CloudLinux/CageFS hosting; update the plugin to version 2.4.8 or later and grep cPanel logs for the documented exploitation indicators.

title: Google exposes China espionage group that’s been lurking in networks undetected since 2023
blurb: Google Threat Intelligence Group disclosed UNC6508, a previously unknown Chinese state-sponsored espionage group that breached US and Canadian targets across medicine, military, academia, and foreign policy, dwelling undetected since at least September 2023. The actor exploited externally facing REDCap research-database servers, then deployed the INFINITERED backdoor to steal administrative credentials, though Google has not determined the initial-access vector.

title: Crooks found a new way to collaborate using Teams – by hiding command-and-control traffic
blurb: Attackers are abusing Microsoft Teams as a covert command-and-control channel, tunneling C2 traffic through legitimate Teams messaging to blend in with normal enterprise collaboration and evade network monitoring. Defenders should inspect Teams traffic for anomalous automation, restrict external federation, and alert on processes spawning Teams API calls outside expected user behavior.

title: SprySOCKS Windows Variant Abuses Kernel Drivers to Evade Detection
blurb: ESET documented a previously undocumented Windows variant of SprySOCKS, a backdoor used by the China-nexus group FishMonger (aka Earth Lusca, Aquatic Panda), deployed against government targets in Honduras, Taiwan, Thailand, and Pakistan during 2023 and 2024. The WIN_DRV build chains two encrypted kernel drivers, DriverLoader (fsdiskbit.sys) and RawWNPF, to hide the backdoor's processes and files by intercepting system calls and to kill security tooling.

title: Chrome and Firefox Updated to Patch Critical, High-Severity Vulnerabilities
blurb: Google and Mozilla shipped updates patching over 70 vulnerabilities across Chrome and Firefox, including seven critical use-after-free flaws in Chrome 149 and 13 high-severity memory safety bugs in Firefox 152, several exploitable for RCE or sandbox escape. None are known to be exploited in the wild, but admins should push the Chrome 149.0.7827.155/.156 and Firefox 152 builds, plus the parallel ESR, Thunderbird, and iOS fixes, before public PoCs emerge.

title: Cordon: Semantic Transactions for Tool-Using LLM Agents
blurb: This paper introduces Cordon, a transactional runtime that stages and validates an LLM agent's irreversible tool effects before commit, replacing per-call guardrails with a task-scoped execution boundary. It executes reversible mutations in shadow state, queues outward actions in an effect outbox, and validates the composed flow before releasing anything. In evaluation, Cordon caught cross-step violations that existing defenses missed and cut irreversible-effect failures while preserving benign task completion at modest approval and latency overhead.

title: Verifying the Rust Standard Library
blurb: A crowdsourced campaign integrated formal-verification tools into the CI of a fork of the Rust standard library, targeting the unsafe code that underpins memory safety but had only been validated through testing and Miri's dynamic checks. The authors report machine-checked proofs ruling out a subset of undefined behaviors, including out-of-bounds access, null and dangling pointer dereferences, and use of uninitialized memory, and frame the unproven remainder as open challenges for the formal-methods community.

title: Google Vertex AI SDK Flaw Let Attackers Hijack Model Uploads via Bucket Squatting
blurb: A flaw in Google Cloud's Vertex AI SDK for Python (fixed in 1.148.0) let an attacker who knew only a victim's public project ID pre-create the predictable default staging bucket, redirecting the victim's model upload to attacker-controlled storage and swapping in a malicious pickle that ran code inside Vertex AI's serving container. Palo Alto Unit 42, which reported the "Pickle in the Middle" bug, saw no in-the-wild exploitation; update the SDK and set an explicit staging_bucket.

title: PARSE: Provenance-Aware Retrieval Sanitization for Professional Domain LLM Agents
blurb: PARSE, a new prompt-injection defense, argues that paraphrasing and other defenses validated on synthetic benchmarks fail on real enterprise documents, where authority language and factual content interleave. Tested on 122 tasks across financial, legal, medical, scientific, and DevOps domains using real SEC filings, Federal Register rules, and GitHub postmortems, PARSE cut attack success from 25.4% to 15.6% at 86.9% utility by classifying sentences by injection likelihood and verifying fact preservation. The authors urge teams to evaluate defenses on domain-matched real documents, not synthetic proxies.

title: Anywhere, Any-Stymie: Remote Activation of Trojan Malware on LiDAR with Modulated Signals
blurb: Researchers demonstrated dormant malware embedded in LiDAR firmware that stays inactive until an attacker triggers it remotely with a modulated optical signal, requiring no hardware or network access at attack time. Once activated, the malware manipulates point clouds in real time to inject false objects or suppress real ones, validated at 300 ft and in drive-by runs at 35 mph against a deployed autonomous vehicle.

title: Joomla, LiteSpeed Vulnerabilities Exploited in Attacks
blurb: CVE-2026-48907, an improper-access flaw in the Joomla Content Editor (JCE Pro before 2.9.99.5), is being actively exploited via public, automated exploit code to upload arbitrary files and achieve unauthenticated PHP code execution, while CVE-2026-54420 in LiteSpeed's cPanel plugin (before 2.4.8) lets FTP or web-shell users symlink their way to root on CloudLinux/CageFS hosts. CISA added both to the KEV catalog with federal patch deadlines of June 18 and 19, and updating closes the entry point but does not remediate already-compromised servers.

title: Structural Role Injection in Handlebars-Templated LLM Prompts: Triple-Brace Interpolation, Delimiter Family, and the Limits of HTML Auto-Escaping
blurb:
This paper shows that Handlebars HTML auto-escaping, the documented safe default in Microsoft Semantic Kernel's prompt templates, fails to stop structural role injection because it rewrites angle brackets but leaves square-bracket, colon, and Markdown role delimiters intact. Across 5760 trials, GPT-3.5 Turbo followed injected task-hijack instructions in 91% of escaped trials while Claude Haiku 4.5 resisted, confirming escaping cannot substitute for separating instruction from data.

(73 words)

title: Microsoft AntiSSRF open-source library helps block server-side request forgery
blurb: Microsoft released AntiSSRF, an open-source MIT-licensed library for .NET and Node.js that validates URLs and outbound connections to block server-side request forgery before applications make requests. Configured via an AntiSSRFPolicy object, it supports IP allow/deny lists, denying unspecified addresses, blocking plain-text HTTP, and Azure Key Vault and Storage domain checks, with samples for HttpClient, Axios, follow-redirects, and node-fetch.

title: Your Privacy My Cloak: Backdoor Attacks on Differentially Private Federated Learning
blurb: RING, a composable attack layer, exploits differential privacy to mask backdoors in federated learning, hitting 90.3% success against six defenses.

title: UK to require ID or face scan before you can make social media accounts
blurb: The UK will ban under-16s from social media starting spring 2027, requiring platforms like Instagram, TikTok, YouTube, and X to age-verify new accounts via government ID or facial age scans. Security and privacy experts warn the checks are trivially bypassed and centralize ID and biometric data into breach-prone honeypots, effectively ending anonymous account creation in the UK.

title: Security and Privacy Prompts in the Wild: What Users Ask LLMs and How LLMs Respond
blurb: A study mined WildChat's 3.2M user-LLM conversations to identify 14,727 security and privacy prompts, the first analysis of real-world S&P questions users pose to chatbots rather than expert-authored FAQs. Across 270 advice-seeking prompts run 10 times each, GPT-5.5 gave "good enough" answers 98% of the time versus Llama 4's 47%, but commercial models sometimes returned contradictory guidance across runs.

title: Seeing Is Not Screening: Multimodal Hidden Instruction Attacks on Agent Skill Scanners
blurb: This paper introduces SkillCamo, a multimodal attack that hides malicious operational instructions inside images bundled with an LLM agent skill while rewriting the surrounding documentation to reference those images as part of a normal workflow, evading scanners that analyze only text, manifests, and source code. The authors show the payload stays recoverable by multimodal agents at execution time and propose ExecScan, an execution-grounded module that jointly inspects docs, code, and visual content to reconstruct hidden behavior chains.

title: Endpoint Security Startup Ent Emerges From Stealth With $100 Million Seed Round
blurb: Ent emerged from stealth with $100M in seed funding, led by Decibel with In-Q-Tel and Sequoia participating, to build endpoint security that interprets user and AI-agent intent in real time before risky actions complete. Founded by RiskIQ veterans Elias Manousos and Brandon Dixon, Ent deploys a lightweight on-device agent that enforces policy and intervenes pre-incident, extending existing EDR, SIEM, SOAR, and IAM stacks.

title: Microsoft says you don’t need another email security tool; experts say, not so fast
blurb: Microsoft's new quarterly benchmark claims Defender for Office 365 catches nearly all malicious email on its own, with integrated third-party ICES partners adding under 0.05% to the catch rate. Microsoft reports a high-severity miss rate of 194 per 1,000 employees versus 478 for Mimecast and 483 for Proofpoint. Experts counter that percentages obscure the volume and severity of what slips through, and that one message is enough to justify defense in depth.

title: Differential Privacy of Gaussian Process Posterior Sampling
blurb: This paper shows that releasing posterior sample paths from a Gaussian process is differentially private on its own, since the sampling randomness substitutes for the noise that standard DP mechanisms add externally. The authors derive explicit Rényi-DP bounds, finding that meaningful privacy hinges on effective ridge regularization, and confirm the predicted leakage with membership-inference attacks. For stronger guarantees, calibrated GP noise serves as an additional tunable privacy knob.

title: ShellGames: Speculative LLM-Driven SSH Deception
blurb: ShellGames is an LLM-based SSH honeypot that aims to sustain credible, long-lived attacker sessions where prior LLM deception falls short. It combines chain-of-thought prompting, memory for state coherency, speculative command execution to cut latency, sandbox routing for complex commands, and subversion detection, reporting 0.898 command accuracy, 0.918 sequence consistency, and 0.95 robustness against probing. A 20-person user study rated its realism comparable to a real shell and ahead of traditional honeypots on perceived command coverage.

title: Bifrost: Hybrid TEE-FHE Inference for Privacy-Preserving Transformer and LLM Serving
blurb: Bifrost is a hybrid TEE-FHE architecture for confidential LLM inference that provisions secrets only to an attested CPU TEE, keeping the accelerator, drivers, and host stack outside the trusted computing base. It uses FHE for ciphertext-only delegation of linear layers while running non-linear, KV-state, and refresh operations inside the TEE. The authors report 9.25x to 9.91x projected latency cuts on GPT-2 and LLaMA 3, with a Bifrost+ prefill/decode split reducing TTFT up to 53.4x.

title: OTRO: Oblivious Tokenization Path with Square-Root ORAM
blurb: Researchers introduced OTRO, an oblivious tokenization path that closes a side-channel gap in confidential LLM serving, where prior work recovered full user prompts from CPU tokenizer memory-access patterns on production Intel TDX. Standard PathORAM mitigations impose roughly 13x tokenizer slowdown and 10-58% higher time-to-first-token. OTRO uses replicated square-root ORAM instances with epoch-based rotation and KV-cache-aware tokenization to cap TTFT overhead at 4.5% while reducing observable leakage, a practical option for teams running LLMs inside CPU and GPU TEEs.

title: US Cracks Down on Anthropic AI Models Amid Abuse Concerns
blurb: Anthropic abruptly suspended all access to its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models after a US export control directive barred foreign nationals from using the technology over abuse concerns. The move strands international customers mid-deployment and signals tightening federal control over frontier model access, a precedent that could extend to other labs facing similar export scrutiny.

title: An Empirical Analysis of AI Slop in Music Streaming
blurb: A new paper analyzing AI-generated "slop" music on streaming platforms found that 93% of AI tracks receive few or no plays, while artists "spray and pray" by flooding multiple genres in hopes of a hit. The authors published their own AI songs through 11 indie distributors with inconsistent, largely unenforced AI policies, and warn that weak, non-robust detection methods will let slop become a self-sustaining shadow industry without industry intervention.

title: Humiliating IIS servers for fun and jail time
blurb: A bug bounty researcher details a methodology for finding and exploiting Microsoft IIS servers, using Shodan and Google dorks to surface forgotten staging boxes, admin panels, and internet-facing internal tools, then probing for common misconfigurations and bypassing WAFs via HTTP parameter pollution. Defenders should audit their external IIS footprint for indexed artifacts like aspnet_client and _vti_bin, decommission stray servers, and confirm WAF rules can't be evaded through duplicate parameters.

title: Kodak confirms data breach claimed by ShinyHunters extortion gang
blurb: Kodak confirmed that an unauthorized third party temporarily accessed and copied a limited amount of company data, and it has engaged external cybersecurity experts and law enforcement to investigate. The ShinyHunters extortion gang claimed responsibility, alleging it stole over 2.2 million records of customer PII and internal corporate data, with a June 18 deadline before it leaks the files.

title: Cybersecurity experts blast US government for restricting Anthropic’s AI models
blurb: Security experts and CISOs criticized recent US export controls limiting access to Anthropic's AI models, calling the restrictions "dangerous" for defensive cybersecurity work. The pushback argues the ban hampers researchers who rely on frontier models for threat analysis and detection, while doing little to constrain well-resourced adversaries who can source comparable capabilities elsewhere.

title: SNAS: A Multi-Layer Defense-in-Depth Architecture for Secure Egress in Sandboxed Workloads
blurb: This paper presents SNAS (Secure Network Access in Snowpark), a production architecture for policy-driven egress control from sandboxed UDF workloads while preserving multi-tenant isolation.
